details.text:
الم

- الم Alif Laam Meem - These are from the Huroof Al-Muqatta’aat (الحُرُوفُ المُقَطَّعات), letters which are cut apart and separated from each other, with which some of the Suwar of the Qur’an begin with. The majority view is that none but Allah knows their meaning although some of the Sahabah and Scholars have provided some explanations for the meanings of these letters. It has been said that these letters represent a challenge to the Arabs in respect to the Qur’an having been revealed in their language and yet they are unable to reproduce the like of it.
Derivative: أَلِيمٌ (Aleem) refers to a pain that is constant and implies a pain that tolerance cannot be built up for.
- “They used to continually lie” the use of كَانَ with the مَضَارِع (Mudaari’ - present tense form) implies continuation of the action.

Derivative: نَبَذَ (Nabadha) means to throw or toss something away and discard it. With contracts carries the meaning of breaking it and defaulting on it. It is used when someone fails to see the importance or significance of the thing he is casting aside.

- “Those (الَّذِينَ) who were given (أُوتُوا) the Book (الْكِتَابَ)” – An insight into this phrase is that Allah uses this expression which is in the passive voice form when the context is negative and uses the active voice form آتَيْنَا (Aataynaa) “We gave”, when the context is positive.

“As if they (كَأَنَّهُمْ) do not know (لَا يَعْلَمُونَ)” – The use of كَأَنَّ (Ka-Anna – “As if”) implies that they do in fact know and they are only acting as if they are unaware of what they are doing.
Derivative: خَيْرٌ literally means “good or goodness” and is used to imply the meaning of “better” when being used to provide a comparison with something else.

Derivative: مَثُوبَةٌ (Mathoobah) means that which has been given as a reward and it can be understood to be an اِسْم مَفْعُول (Ism Maf’ool) in the case where a verb is implicitly understood to proceed it like: “They will be rewarded with a reward (مَثُوبَةٌ (Mathoobah))”. It can also be understood to be an اِسْم مَصْدَر (Ism Masdar) in which case it can retain its meaning as “That which has been given as a reward” without the need of a verb.

Derivative: رَاعِ is the command form of the verb رَاعَى – يُرَاعِي – مُرَاعَاة and رِعَايَة which means to observe the other, to give him time, to take care of his affairs. رَاعِنَا with the pronoun of نا (naa – us) attached to the command رَاعِ (observe or give time) was similar to a word that the Jews would insult the Messenger ﷺ with. When the companions used the phrase رَاعِنَا (Raa’inaa) when they wanted the Messenger to help them understand a matter, the Jews exploited that expression to belittle him ﷺ.

- انظُر (Unzhur) is the command form from نَظَرَ and implies the meanings of: “Look and wait”. انظُرْنَا (Unzhurnaa) is an alternative manner of asking to be given some attention that cannot be manipulated like the word رَاعِنَا (Raa’inaa) was.

Derivative: مَا in Arabic is not just a negation but also implies refutation. It is used to refute a notion or argument that previously exists. It is a stronger form of negation implying “Not at all”.

Derivative: مَا in Arabic is not just a negation but also implies refutation. It is used to refute a notion or argument that previously exists. It is a stronger form of negation implying “Not at all”.

مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِّنْهَا أَوْ مِثْلِهَا ۗ أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

- Ustadh Nu’man Ali Khan explains that the main context of this verse is the People of the Book following on from previous verses. He explains that the abrogation (النَّسْخ An-Naskh) referred to here is the abrogation of some parts of the previous revelations which have become abrogated by what Allah revealed to His Messenger ﷺ. In addition, what has been mentioned to be forgotten is also from the previous revelations. This view however is contrary to the widely held view amongst the first Muslims and scholars of Islam that the context of this abrogation is the revelation that came to Muhammad ﷺ itself, in reference to commands which were abrogated and replaced with others and verses which were initially revealed and then later removed from the Mus’haf.

Derivative: نَصِيرٍ is a name of a description (اِسْمُ الصِّفَة - Ism us-Sifah) which implies a permanent quality. It comes from the word نُصْرَة (Nusrah) or نَصْر (Nasr) which means help against oppression. نَصِيرٍ (Naseer) implies the meaning of a helper against oppression.

Derivative: تَسْأَلُوا is from سَأَلَ which means to ask and is used for asking a question. Here, the asking can refer to making demands by asking for things or about things which were intended to question the authenticity of the Messenger ﷺ or undermine him. It can also refer to asking too many questions about things, a matter which Bani Isra’eel were reproached for and the Muslims warned about.
Derivative: اصْفَحُوا can mean to turn the page and overlook. It is similar in meaning to العَفْو (Al-‘Afw) to pardon and can imply the meaning in this context “to avoid reproaching and confronting”.

Derivative: اعْفُوا comes from العَفْو (Al-‘Afw) which means to pardon. It is suggested that العَفْو (Al-‘Afw) is something that is done in a loving manner which can be seen in certain contexts. However, this does not fit with the context here. Pardoning linguistically means to leave the punishment and the majority of the scholars have stated that اعْفُوا means here “to leave them be” i.e., don’t go after them or pay attention to them.

Derivative: تُقَدِّمُوا from قَدَّمَ means to advance and to send forward or ahead, referring to the deeds that are undertaken and sent forward for the future to receive reward for them at a later time.
Derivative: أَمَانِيُّ (Amaaniy) refers to wishful thoughts which are not based upon evidence or knowledge.

“And it is not (وَلَا) them (هُمْ) who will be grieving (يَحْزَنُونَ)” – If هُمْ (Hum – them) was omitted from the statement it would mean: “And they will not grieve”. The addition of هُمْ (Hum – them) here adds the implication: “It is not “them” who will be grieving i.e. it will be others who will be grieving”.

- “Whoever (مَنْ) surrenders/devotes/commits (أَسْلَمَ) his face (وَجْهَهُ) to Allah (لِلَّهِ)” – The origin of أَسْلَمَ is to surrender and submit. His face (وَجْهَهُ) is used to refer to the person as it represents the main feature of a person. It can also refer to the source of his pride or that where he has been most honoured. “His face” can also imply the prostration which is the position which exemplifies man surrendering to Allah. The meaning of the statement is therefore: “Whoever surrenders himself to Allah i.e., by following His commands and worshipping Him alone with pure sincerity”.

- الَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ “Those who don’t know” refers to the Mushrikeen (Polytheists) i.e., those who unlike the Jews and Christians do not have a source of knowledge in terms of a book and scriptures to refer to.

Derivative: خَائِفِينَ means “in a state of fear”. This fear being alluded to here can be the fear of Allah associated with entering His house due to the honour of the houses of Allah. It can also allude to being “in a state of fear” from Allah or even the believers due the crimes they have committed.

Derivative: مَسَاجِدَ (Masaajid) is the plural of مَسْجِد (Masjid) which is an اِسْم ظِرْف (Ism Zharf) which refers to a place and time. It comes from سَجْدَة (Sajdah) which means prostration. مَسْجِد (Masjid) therefore implies both the place and time of the Sajdah i.e., of the prayer. And مَسَاجِدَ (Masaajid) can refer to both the places of worship and the times that the worship is to take place, like during the Hajj.

- it Literally means “The face of Allah” in the case where وَجْهُ اللَّهِ “Face of Allah” is an expression to mean “The pleasure of Allah” just as وَجْهُ also means “direction”. وَجْهُ اللَّهِ (Wajhullah) can therefore imply “The direction of Allah”.
Derivative: كُلُّ (Kullu) normally comes attached to something in the إِضَافَة (form). When كُلٌّ (Kullun) is used in this form with Tanween ٌ it means everything.

Derivative: قَانِتُونَ comes from قُنُوت (Qunoot) which means to be submissive, subservient and humble before one’s master. It is a type of obedience accompanied by humbleness and an enthusiastic willingness to serve. A قَانِت (Qaanit) is ready and willing to immediately obey his master.

- سُبْحَانَهُ – سُبْحَانَ (Subhaana) comes from سَبَّحَ – تَسْبِيح. Its meaning is “The declaration of Allah’s perfection and His being free of any fault or deficiency”. It is related to the root letters of سَبَحَ which means to swim upon the surface of the water and implies that Allah’s status must be maintained high and not be permitted to be lowered (i.e., below the water). سُبْحَانَهُ implies the meaning: “He is far too perfect (for such a thing to be considered)” i.e., in relation to what has been claimed and associated with Him. It is used in response to a claim that is made which seeks to lower the perfection of Allah just as it used to declare His perfection.
Derivative: بَدِيعُ means the One who initiates and originates creation, creating out of nothing.
- “Be (كُن), and then it is (فَيَكُونُ)” – This phrase explaining Allah’s manner of creation is frequently used in the Qur’an response to the claim of the Christians that ‘Isa (as) is His son.

- “Those who (الَّذِينَ) don’t know (لَا يَعْلَمُونَ)” is the descriptive phrase used here and other places in Al-Baqarah to refer to the Mushrikeen (polytheists) of Makkah i.e., those who did not have a Book or scripture.

- “Already (قَدْ) We have made clear (بَيَّنَّا) the Aayaat (الْآيَاتِ) for a nation (لِقَوْمٍ) that wishes to be convinced (يُوقِنُونَ)” – The use of the verb “يُوقِنُونَ” to describe the state of the nation (قَوْم - Qawm) instead of the noun “مُوقِنُون” indicates that the process f being convinced is ongoing and that they have not yet reached the state of “conviction” but are rather active in treading its path. As such يُوقِنُونَ does not imply those who already have conviction but rather those who are sincerely seeking to attain it.

Derivative: الْجَحِيمِ (Al-Jaheem) is the name that has been given for the hellfire. The word جَهْم (Jahm) is used in the Arabic language to refer to the face that is scowling and has harshness in it. It was used to describe the face of a lion and has been said that the hell fire (Al-Jaheem) has such a scowling glare.
- أَلَا is an expression that implies: “No, you should know” or “There is no doubt about it” and is used to alert or draw attention to a fact.

“Those whom (الَّذِينَ) We gave them (آتَيْنَاهُمُ) the Book (الْكِتَابَ)” – آتَيْنَاهُمُ “We gave them” is in the active voice and is used when the context is positive is being stated in contrast to the statement الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ “Those who were given the Book” which uses the passive voice and is used when the context is negative. This follows the pattern of Allah distancing Himself from them when expressing disappointment in the People who were given the Book or reproaching them on the one hand and attaching Himself to them when commending them or discussing the favours that He gave to them, on the other hand.

Derivative: نِعْمَة (Ni’mah) refers to a favour or blessing and has come in the singular form here. It could be in reference to the main favour that Allah bestowed upon Bani Isra’eel or it could be used as a name of a category (اِسْمُ الجِنْس – Ism ul-Jins) where the singular noun is used to refer to everything that falls under its category. In that case it would refer to every favour or all favours that Allah bestowed upon them.

Derivative: إِمَامًا comes from the verb أَمَّ – يَؤُمُّ which means to have intent and purpose. An أُمَّة (Ummah) is collective of people unified by a purpose. An إِمَام (Imaam) is someone the people follow. He leads the people and gives them purpose and direction.

Derivative: أَتَمَّ and أَكْمَلَ are the words used in the Qur’an with the meaning of “to complete”. أَكْمَلَ (Akmala) normally relates to completing something that is time related whilst أَتَمَّ (Atamma) means to perfect something and is related to the completion of the thing that is being done to. If that thing is a duty, then the meaning of أَتَمَّ (Atamma) is “to fulfil to perfection”.
Derivative: عَهْد (‘Ahd) refers to a covenant or promise. The meaning of covenant is used in relation to the People of the Book. Here the meaning of promise is more appropriate.

Derivative: ابْتَلَىٰ is in origin from بَلَى which means to test or put to the test and trial. ابْتَلَىٰ (Ibtalaa) implies a stronger trial and test than بَلَى (Balaa). It is also from the words in Arabic which can also hold an opposite meaning. بَلَى can mean أَنْعَمَ which means to bestow a blessing or favour (نِعْمَة – Ni’mah). There is also an implication that something can start as a test and trial but end up being a blessing.
Derivative: كَلِمَاتٍ literally means word and has been understood in this context to refer to instructions or commands. It has been left open and general without specification.
Derivative: ذُرِّيَّة means progeny and refers to the descendants that follow generation after generation.

- “He fulfilled or completed (أَتَمَّ) them (هُنَّ)” – “هُنَّ” (Them) is a pronoun used for humans however here it is referring to كَلِمَاتٍ (Kalimaat – Words) which is non-human and usually would have the pronoun of هَا (Haa) to refer to them. The usage of هُنَّ (Them) here is for the purpose of تَفْخِيم (Tafkheem) which is employed to magnify the matter and give it extra weight.
Derivative: مَثَابَةً refers to a place that people often come back to.

- “And remember when Ibrahim raised the foundations of Al-Bait and Isma’eel” – Both Ibrahim (as) and Isma’eel (as) are the doers in this statement but Ibrahim was placed directly following the verb whilst Isma’eel was placed at the end of the statement after the object of the verb. This implies that Ibrahim (as) had the primary role and that Isma’eel (as) was his assistant.
details.arabic_part: وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَاهِيمُ الْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ الْبَيْتِ وَإِسْمَاعِيلُ

details.text: - رَبَّنَا “Our Master (or Lord)” – رَبَّ has a Fat’hah َ attached to the ب (Baa’) at the end. It comes in this form (Ther Mansoob form) because it is مُنادَى (Munaadaa) something that is being called for or to and is used with the supplication.

Derivative: يَتْلُو is from تَلَا and has two meanings: One is to read (or recite) and the other is to follow. The use of the word يَتْلُوا (To read) implies the existence of revelation or a Book that will be read or recited from. يَتْلُو implies the act of conveying and spreading the revealed message.
Derivative: ابْعَثْ is the command form of بَعَثَ which is used in this context to imply appointing and designating someone and elevating someone in terms of standing and status.
Derivative: الْكِتَابَ (Al-Kitaab) refers to the revealed book and was also used to refer in classical Arabic to “The Law”.

Derivative: يُعَلِّمُ is from عَلَّمَ – تَعْلِيم which means to teach and implies something that takes some time to learn in contrast to the act of informing i.e., it refers to the process of departing knowledge so that it is absorbed. يُعَلِّمُ implies teaching and imparting knowledge and understanding to those who have been drawn to the message that had been recited and conveyed.

Derivative: الْحِكْمَةَ (Al-Hikmah) means “The wisdom”. It has been said to refer to the understanding of Al-Kitaab, the rulings that were revealed, to refer to the Sunnah, in the case where Al-Kitaab and Al-Hikmah refer to the Qur’an and the Sunnah, and it also been said that both Al-Kitaab and Al-Hikmah refer to the Qur’an.
Derivative: يُزَكِّي is from زَكَّى – تَزْكِيَة (Tazkiyah) which means to cleanse and purify from impurities.

Derivative: اصْطَفَيْنَا is from اِصْطَفَى which means to choose or select based on a recognition of purity and selecting that which is most pure, in the case where its origin صَفَا implies purity. It also implies a choice that has been made without any other influences and has only been made because it’s the sole choice of the one choosing.

Derivative: يَرْغَبُ comes from رَغِبَ which changes its meaning depending on the preposition that follows it. رَغِبَ attached to فِي or إِلَى implies to incline towards something, to want, wish for and have a like for it. رَغِبَ with عَنْ means to incline away from it and to have a dislike or distaste for it. The meaning of يَرْغَبُ with عَن here implies to turn away from or have a dislike for or an aversion.
Derivative: سَفِهَ means “to fool”. سَفِهَ نَفْسَهُ means “fooled himself”.
Derivative: مِلَّة has been said to refer to the Deen and the Sharee’ah, the foundations of the Deen and Sharee’ah. It has also been said to refer to the nation and also legacy i.e., of Ibrahim (as).

Derivative: أَسْلَمْتُ “I have submitted” – It is in the past tense. The present tense of أُسْلِمُ is used for both the present and future which can imply some delay in terms of implementation. However, the pat tense of أَسْلَمْتُ “I have submitted” removes any delay from the statement.

- “He has chosen (اصْطَفَىٰ) for you (لَكُمُ)” – When the verb اصْطَفَىٰ, which means “to choose”, is used with لِ “for” like in the statement اصْطَفَىٰ لَكُمُ “He has chosen for you”, it implies something has been chosen as a favour, in contrast to when اصْطَفَىٰ is used with عَلَى “Upon” like in the statement اصْطَفَىٰ عَلِيْكم “He has chosen upon you” which implies something has been chosen as a burden.

- It literally means “From (مِن) after me (بَعْدِي)” and is an expression to mean “After I am gone and deceased”. The use of مِن (Min) preceding بَعْدِي (Ba’diy - After me) implies some or much time after and not immediately after.

Derivative: مِلَّة (Millah) has been understood to mean a nation and a legacy. It has the implication of affinity and affection connected to the legacy. مِلَّة (Millah) has been understood by most scholars of Tafsir to be a reference to the Deen.

Derivative: حَنِيفًا (Haneef) means to be not distracted by something else and have singular focus and devotion to one thing. It has been understood to mean focused and inclines solely to the Deen whilst shunning and having an aversion towards all other false beliefs and practises.

Derivative: مَا differs from other forms of negation in Arabic and is used in response to something to negate it and correct it, like a false assumption. It is also from the strongest forms of negation in the Arabic language.

Derivative: نُفَرِّقُ is from فَرَّقَ – تَفْرِيق which means to make a distinction. It refers here to a distinction in respect to believing in the Prophets in the case where we do not make any distinction تَفْرِيق (Tafreeq) between any of them. This differs from تَفْضِيل (Tafdeel) which refers to preference given amongst the Prophets in terms of some having been given more favours or more special favours or raised in degrees above others by Allah.

- In this Aayah it states: “What (مَا) was revealed (أُنزِلَ) to us (إِلَيْنَا)” whilst مَا أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا “What (مَا) was revealed (أُنزِلَ) upon us (عَلَيْنَا)” is also used in the Qur’an. The difference between the use of إِلَى Ilaa – To) and عَلَى (‘Alaa – Upon) is that the latter (عَلَى) when used carries the implication of obligation.

Derivative: صِبْغَةَ (Sibghah) refers to colour and dye. It is the result and effect of having dye put on something where it becomes emersed in that colour. This Sibghah here is in reference to the Deen and the effect that the Deen has on the person who adopts and submits to it.

- “We (نَحْنُ) to Him (لَهُ) are worshipping or enslaving ourselves (عَابِدُونَ)” – The advancement of “to Him” (لَهُ) in the sentence structure implies “Our worshipping is “only” to Him” and that we are not worshipping anything besides him unlike the others who claim to be following guidance.

Derivative: وَسَطًا (Wasat) can refer to something that is lies in the middle between things and imply a balance. It can also carry the meaning of being best of something just as it can refer to being just and upright. According to these meanings, an أُمَّةً (Ummah) that is وَسَطًا (Wasat) can be understood to mean “An Ummah that is the best and just”.
Derivative: إِيمَانَ (Eemaan) means “Belief” however here it refers to the prayers (صَلَوَات) and this indicates to the close connection between Iman and Salah.
Derivative: رَءُوفٌ implies the meaning of being compassionate whilst being completely understanding and appreciative of what someone is going through.

الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ يَعْرِفُونَهُ كَمَا يَعْرِفُونَ أَبْنَاءَهُمْ ۖ وَإِنَّ فَرِيقًا مِّنْهُمْ لَيَكْتُمُونَ الْحَقَّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ

- “Those who we gave them the Book know (or recognise) it or him (يَعْرِفُونَهُ) just as they know or recognise their (own) children. And no doubt a group from them conceal the truth whilst they know (i.e., knowingly)”. That which those who had been given the Book know or recognise has been mentioned by a pronoun “هُ” which can mean “it” or “him”. This has been understood to refer to the Qur’an, or to the Messenger ﷺ or to the truthfulness of the turning in the direction (Qiblah) of Al-Masjid Al-Haraam. One contemporary Mufassir summarised this by stating: “That the Rabbis of the Jews and scholars of the Christians know or recognise the truthfulness of the message of the Prophet ﷺ and they know that his turning towards Al-Bait ul-Haraam is a truth”.

Derivative: الْحَقُّ (Al-Haqq – The truth) – The حّقَ (Haqq – Truth) here refers to the Qur’an or more generally what came to the Messenger ﷺ i.e., The Qur’an or what came to the Messenger ﷺ is the truth from your Lord”. It can also be a reference to the Qiblah being towards Masjid Al-Haraam i.e., the change of the Qiblah is the Haqq (truth) from your Lord.
Derivative: وِجْهَة (Wijhah) means direction and can refer to the Qiblah i.e., direction of prayer, or more generally the direction in terms of life and worship.

Derivative: كُلٍّ (Kull) when used by itself and not in an إِضافَة (Idaafah) construction implies the meaning of the whole or entirety of something, or everyone. Here it has been understood to be in reference to everyone or to every Ummah.

Derivative: يَعْمَهُونَ comes from عَمِهَ which means to be blind in the heart in contrast to عَمِيَ which means blind in the eyes i.e., blinded from the truth and guidance. It can also mean to wander around in a blind state. It can also imply wavering and being in a state of uncertainty.

Derivative: الْحِكْمَةَ (Al-Hikmah) can refer to maturity in knowledge and understanding where it is practised and acted upon. It has also been said by scholars of Tafsir that it refers to the rulings contained in the Book just as it has been said that it refers to the Sunnah.
Derivative: الْكِتَابَ means “The Book” (i.e., Al-Qur’an) and can also refer to “The Law”.
Derivative: يُزَكِّي is from زَكَّى – تَزْكِيَة. تَزْكِيَة (Tazkiyah) means to cleanse and purify from impurities and covers the purification of both the heart and the mind.

- كَمَا (Kamaa) is commonly translated as “Just as” or “Just like” and the purpose of its usage is to compare one situation to another. It can be linked to the statement in the previous Verse: لِأُتِمَّ نِعْمَتِي عَلَيْكُمْ “So that I can perfect (or complete) my favour upon you” in the case where what is mentioned after كَمَا (Kamaa) falls under Allah perfecting His favour upon the believers just like the changing of the Qiblah was part of completing that favour.

Derivative: تَكْفُرُونِ is from كَفَرَ which means to be grateful or to disbelieve. When followed by the preposition بِ it implies disbelief in something or its denial. Here it implies ingratitude which refers to the denial of a favour and it is the opposite of شُكْر (gratitude) which is also mentioned in the Aayah.
Derivative: الصَّبْرِ (Sabr) refers to patient perseverance and steadfastness in respect to maintaining obedience to Allah, fulfilling His commands and facing trials and tests.
Derivative: اسْتَعِينُوا is from اِسْتَعَانَ which means to seek help and assistance to accomplish something or a mission and objective that you are already engaged in or have set for yourself.

- “Seek help (اسْتَعِينُوا) with patience and prayer (بِالصَّبْرِ وَالصَّلَاةِ)” – The connection between الصَّبْرِ (Sabr – Patient perseverance) and الصَّلَاةِ (As-Salaah - Prayer) is that it is the Salaah that is the key to the attainment and maintenance of the Sabr i.e., Sabr is accomplished by Salaah (Prayer) and Dhikr (Remembrance).
Derivative: الثَّمَرَاتِ means fruits and can imply crops and return on investments, just as it has been said to refer to offspring.

Derivative: الْأَنفُسِ refers to the lives of people. A loss of which means death and it has also been said to refer to the hypocrites in the sense that they represent a loss of manpower and are more a hindrance than a benefit.

- “With something (بِشَيْءٍ) from or of (مِّنَ)”. The addition of بِشَيْءٍ مِّنَ in this verse can imply that the believers will be tested in something of fear, hunger etc. in the sense that this something can be from a variety of sources. بِشَيْءٍ مِّنَ can also imply “by some amount of” i.e., you will be tested by at least a small amount of these things.
Derivative: الْمُهْتَدُونَ is the plural of المُهْتَدي who is the one who is truly guided and committed to guidance and is stronger than مَهْدِي which refers to the one who is guided.

Derivative: شَاكِرٌ is the one who is grateful however that meaning is inappropriate with Allah تعالى. As such a closer meaning is to be appreciative in the sense that He rewards those who undertake the act.

Derivative: شَعَائِرِ is the plural of شَعِيرَة. شَعَائِرِ (Sha’aair) are things which symbolise something powerful or of great significance and conjure thoughts and ideas associated with them or represented by them. The شَعَائِرِ of Al-Hajj are its features that stand out to the senses which Allah has made signs for His obedience and the places of His rituals and worship.
Derivative: اللَّاعِنُونَ means “Those who curse” and can refer to the Angels and people; all those who curse them and supplicate against them.
Derivative: مُهْتَدِينَ is the plural of مُهْتَد (Muhtad) and refers to someone who makes the effort to be guided and to commit to guidance.

Derivative: بَيَّنُوا is from بَيَّنَ which means to make evident and clear. It means here “They made clear that which they had been concealing” which was mentioned in the previous Aayah. It also explains how they rectified their situation (أَصْلَحُوا) i.e., by clarifying what they had concealed from the clear evidences and guidance.

- “I will accept their repentance” – Here أَتُوبُ “I will accept repentance” and it is in the first person. In the previous verse where Allah was cursing them, He used the third person يَلْعَنُهُمُ اللَّهُ “Allah curses them”. This can imply a greater closeness in respect to His accepting the repentance of His servants who repent to Him and rectify their ways when compared to His cursing of them when they perpetrate the sin of concealing His clear evidences and guidance. This is then followed by His statement وَأَنَا التَّوَّابُ “And I am At-Tawwaab (the One who keeps accepting Tawbah)” all of which implies that the doors to Allah’s repentance are always open and that he is الرَّحِيمُ (Always merciful). These statements and their implications act as a great encouragement and incentive for people to sincerely repent to Allah.
- “Those (أُولَٰئِكَ), upon them is (عَلَيْهِمْ) the curse (لَعْنَةُ) of Allah (اللَّهِ)” – This is a noun-based sentence (Jumlah Ismiyah) and the noun لَعْنَةُ (La’nah – Curse) implies permanence.

Derivative: يُنظَرُونَ can be the passive form of نَظَرَ (to look) which would mean that they will not be “looked at”. Or it can be the passive form of أَنْظَرَ which means to be reprieved or given respite i.e., time and opportunity to make amends or to escape. The majority have understood the word يُنظَرُونَ to mean that they won’t being given any respite.

Derivative: يَعْقِلُونَ comes from عَقَلَ in origin holds the meaning of tying something. It was used to describe the tying of a woman’s hair, for the cord used to tie camels and the man’s turban around his head which is called عِقَال (‘Iqaal). It was a means of restraint and control. The عَقْل (‘Aql) referring to the intellect and mind was paralleled with something that constrains. The intellect is used to constrain and tie the emotions down in order to reach sound conclusions. The Arabs described the person as being عَاقِل (‘Aaqil) and in possession of عَقْل (‘Aql) if he was able to think after placing his emotions under control i.e., with a clear head. يَعْقِلُونَ implies “those who engage in thought with clear minds”.

Derivative: ظَلَمُوا is from ظَلَمَ – ظُلْمٌ – ظُلْمٌ (Zhulm) in origin means placing something in other than its right place. From this the meaning of wronging somebody, oneself or something has been derived in addition to the meaning of oppressing others and transgressing against them. ظَلَمُوا means “Those who commit wrong”. Here those who place there love in the wrong place have been described as committing wrong.

Derivative: أَندَادًا (Andaad) is the plural of نِدٌّ (Nidd) and means rivals. Here the rivals are those whom people give their love to instead of to Allah and includes people whom others dedicate their love and affection to, follow and imitate.
Derivative: حَسَرَاتٍ is the plural of حَسْرَة and refers to multiple sources of regret, grief and sorrow and it implies sorrowful regret upon sorrowful regret.

- The command to “Eat from what is in the earth that is Halal (permissible) and Tayyib (Good and pure)” is not restricted to food but includes all matters related to consumption including wealth and financial or economic matters.
details.arabic_part: كُلُوا مِمَّا فِي الْأَرْضِ حَلَالًا طَيِّبًا

details.text: Derivative: الْفَحْشَاءِ refers to shamelessness, indecency and immoral and lewd behaviour. It is usually connected to lust and desires. It refers to that which Islam classifies as being repugnant and heinous including major sins such as Zina, consuming alcohol, murder etc.
Derivative: السُّوءِ refers to evil and bad things i.e., sinful acts.

- “He (i.e., Shaytan) only commands you (إِنَّمَا يَأْمُرُكُم) with evil (بِالسُّوءِ) and immorality (وَالْفَحْشَاءِ)” – الْفَحْشَاءِ (Al-Fahshaa’ - Immorality) falls under السُّوءِ (As-Soo’- Evil sinful acts) but is mentioned to highlight it in particular.

Derivative: ظُلُمَاتٍ is the plural of ظُلْمَة (Zhulmah) which means darkness. The plural can be used to refer to the different shades or varieties of darkness and it has also been said to imply the intensity of the darkness with layers upon layers of darkness on top of each other i.e., a deep darkness.

مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي اسْتَوْقَدَ نَارًا فَلَمَّا أَضَاءَتْ مَا حَوْلَهُ ذَهَبَ اللَّهُ بِنُورِهِمْ وَتَرَكَهُمْ فِي ظُلُمَاتٍ لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ

- The الَّذِي “the one who” is in the singular form whilst the remainder of the verse is in the plural form. This could indicate that the one who lit the fire is different to those whom the light was taken away from. It could also indicate that one person from a group lit it, or that الَّذِي carries the meaning of الَّذِينَ which has been used in other places in the Qur’an and language i.e., the plural of which الَّذِي which means “those whom”.

Derivative: أَلْفَيْنَا from أَلْفَى which means to find come upon. It is used with عَلى (Upon) and refers to habits, traditions, customs and practises. أَلْفَيْنَا means “We found” in the context of what “we found” our forefathers doing, believing and following.
Derivative: بُكْمٌ (Bukm) is the plural of (Abkam) which refers to somebody who is unable to speak (or mute).
Derivative: عُمْيٌ (‘Umy) is the plural of أَعْمَى (A’amaa) which refers to someone who is blind.
Derivative: يَنْعِقُ means to shout and cry out and is used to describe how a sheep herder communicates with his flock by making a lot of noise to move them.
Derivative: صُمٌّ (Summ) is the plural of أَصَمّ (Asamm) which refers to someone who is deaf.

- لَا يَعْقِلُونَ in This context means that those being discussed are unable to use their minds to understand just Like cattle are incapable of comprehending the meaning of the speech of the sheep herder.

- دُعَاءً وَنِدَاءً (Du’aa’an and Nidaa’an) can be translated as “Calls and cries” and is a figure of speech to refer to just “hearing noise” i.e., something that is unintelligible or isn’t understood. دُعَاءً (Du’aa’) is a lower call than وَنِدَاءً (Nidaa’).
Derivative: تَعْبُدُونَ can come from the meaning of عَبْد (‘Abd - a slave) or the meaning عَبَاَدَة (‘Ibaadah - Worship). تَعْبُدُونَ can therefore imply both “You worship” and “You are enslaved”.

Derivative: بَاغٍ refers to someone who engages in wilful defiance out of rebelliousness i.e., to defiantly do something with the knowledge that it is wrong. It can also refer to someone who desires something.
Derivative: عَادٍ refers to someone who is hostile towards something or someone who transgresses against the limits.

“Then there is no (فَلَا) sin (إِثْمَ) upon him (عَلَيْهِ)” - لَا إِثْمَ with a single Fat’hah (َ) on إِثْمَ means that this is لَا نَافِيَة الجِنْس (Laa Naafiyat ul-Jins) which means the negation of the whole category of the noun. This therefore implies that “There is no sin upon him at all”.

- مَا أَصْبَرَهُمْ utilises one of the styles of تَعَجُّب (expressing wonder) in the Arabic language and carries the meaning of “How patient and persevering they are”. It implies that they are doing everything possible to enter the fire!
Derivative: الْمُتَّقُونَ is from اِتَّقَى which means to protect yourself out of fear. المُتَّقِي (Al-Muttaqi) is the one protects himself.
Derivative: الْبَأْسِ (Al-Ba’s) refers to war and battle. حِينَ الْبَأْسِ “At the time of war or battle” means “When in the midst of battle”.
Derivative: الضَّرَّاءِ has been said to refer to sickness and harms or difficulties that affect or impact the person.
Derivative: الْبَأْسَاءِ has been said to refer to severe times and severe poverty which causes suffering.

Derivative: الصَّابِرِينَ (As-Saabireena) means “Those who patiently persevere or are steadfast”. It implies the meaning of stability, constancy and consistency in all situations, to remain steadfast and persevere in both times of difficulty and ease. It is in the Mansoob (accusative) form instead of صَابِرُونَ (Saabiroona) because of what is known in Arabic as نَصْب عَلَى المَدْح (Nasb ‘Alaa l-Mad’h) which means that out of praise and commendation it has been made to take the Nasb or Mansoob (accusative) form of ِينَ (eena) at the end. It is used to highlight the word to emphasis it and implies the significance and importance of this in the context of the verse.

Derivative: الزَّكَاةَ (Zakaah) in origin in the Arabic language means purity. The Zakaah of wealth is an act of worship to purify someone’s wealth and property and is an obligation in contrast to the giving of wealth mentioned earlier in the verse which is voluntary or done by one’s own accord.

Derivative: الْمَسَاكِينَ is the plural of مِسْكِين (Miskeen) and some have argued that it comes from a combination of two verbs: سَكَنَ (Sakana) and مَسَكَ (Masaka) and their أَفْعَلَ (Af’ala) forms i.e., أَسْكَنَ (Askana) and أَمْسَكَ (Amsaka). The first implies being settled in a situation and the second implies being stuck in a situation. The Miskeen is the one who is stuck in his situation of being in poverty and in need and that situation has settled upon him. The word مَسْكَنَة (Maskanah) refers to the situation of being stuck in poverty or misery.

Derivative: الْبِرَّ (Al-Birr) is a word that implies expansiveness in goodness or doing good. It is said is a word encompassing every good act, every act of obedience and every act that gains closeness to Allah. For this reason, الْبِرَّ (Al-Birr) has commonly been translated as righteousness. الْبِرَّ (Al-Birr) can be understood to imply “That which goodness looks like or is demonstrated by”. البارّ (Al-Baarr) is the one who possesses this goodness or righteousness and its plural is أَبْرَار (Abraar).
- أَقَامَ الصَّلَاةَ means “He established the Salaah (prayer)” – The establishment implies that the Salaah is established by an institution and not just performed.
- ابْنَ السَّبِيلِ (Ibn us-Sabeel) literally means “The son of the path” and is an expression used in Arabic to refer to the traveller and implies the person who is from out of town.
- ذَوِي الْقُرْبَىٰ literally means “those who are closest or have the closest relations (with you)” and it is understood to refer to relatives or those related by blood.

- “And he gives his wealth عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِ” – عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِ has been interpreted in two ways. The first is that he gives wealth based upon his love for Allah, in the case where the pronoun ه is in reference to Allah. The second is that he gives wealth against or despite his love i.e., even though he has love for wealth, he still gives from it. The second view is the view held by the majority.

- فِي الرِّقَابِ (Fi r-Riqaab) literally means “in the necks” and is an expression to mean “for the sake of slaves” in the case where a neck رَقَبَة (Raqabah) was used in Arabic to refer to a slave. It has also been said to refer to those who are in a situation like a chain is around their necks and they need alleviation from that, like for example prisoners of war who can be ransomed.
Derivative: الْقَتْلَى (Al-Qatlaa) is the plural of قَتِيل (Qateel) which refers to the person who has been killed.
Derivative: الْقِصَاصُ refers to the law of equal retribution in respect to killing and infliction of wounds.

- “The free person (الْحُرُّ) for the free person (بِالْحُرِّ) and the slave (وَالْعَبْدُ) for the slave (بِالْعَبْدِ) and the female (وَالْأُنثَىٰ) for the female (بِالْأُنثَىٰ)” – The use of Alif Meem (ال) indicates that it specifically the one who committed the crime of killing who will have the law of retribution applied upon him alone. And the pairing of the free for the free, the slave for the slave and the female for the female indicates to the like for like and equal fair application of the law of retribution.
details.arabic_part: الْحُرُّ بِالْحُرِّ وَالْعَبْدُ بِالْعَبْدِ وَالْأُنثَىٰ بِالْأُنثَىٰ

details.text: - “And especially for you (وَلَكُمْ), in Al-Qisaas (فِي الْقِصَاصِ) there is life (حَيَاةٌ)” – The implication of “especially” is due to the advancement of the word لَكُمْ “for you” I the sentence structure providing it with prominence. The word حَيَاةٌ “life” here means the preservation of life with the meaning that the result and impact of the application of Al-Qisaas is that life will be preserved and lives saved by it acting as a deterrent within the societal context.

- “Deaf (صُمٌّ), mute (بُكْمٌ), blind (عُمْيٌ)” These are all plurals and they do not have a وَ “and” separating the words which implies that those being described have all three attributes at the same time as if they are one.
- بِالْمَعْرُوفِ (Bil-Ma’ruf) literally means “By that which is known” and it is used to mean: According to that which is known to be reasonable and acceptable according to the norms of society.
Derivative: جَنَفًا means to be inclined, biased and partial. In this context it refers to the one leaving a will not being fair in it and has deviated away from what is right in respect to it.

Derivative: الصِّيَامِ comes from صَامَ – يَصُومُ and means to fast. It was originally used by the Arabs to describe the process of preparing their horses for war as they needed to be conditioned by fasting in order to endure in the desert environment. As such the word has connotations in the minds of the Arabs which are linked to engaging in warfare. It can also imply that fasting is connected to the preparation of the believers and readying them to undertake what is required from them.
Derivative: كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ – “Has been written upon you” is an expression to mean “It has been written upon you as law” i.e., it has been prescribed upon you.

أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَاتٍ ۚ فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ ۚ وَعَلَى الَّذِينَ يُطِيقُونَهُ فِدْيَةٌ طَعَامُ مِسْكِينٍ ۖ فَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُ ۚ وَأَن تَصُومُوا خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

There is a difference of opinion whether this verse relates to fasting that was obligated prior to Ramadan in the case where a limited number of days refers to the few days that were obligated, or that it relates to the month of Ramdhan and the obligation of fasting it.

- أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَاتٍ means “Days that are numbered” and is an expression to mean “A limited number of days”. مَّعْدُودَاتٍ “Ma’doodaat” is in the جَمْع القِلَّة (Jam’u l-Qillah – The plural of a few or little). مَّعْدُودَاتٍ “Ma’doodaat” therefore implies a few days and it is said that it means less than ten.
Derivative: أُجِيبُ from أَجَابَ – يُجِيبُ – إِجَابَة which means to answer and respond immediately.
Derivative: إِذَا “When” – When إِذَا (Idhaa) is used it implies the future even when the verb following it is in the past tense. That contrasts with إِذ (Idh) which implies the past when used.
Derivative: عَفَا means to pardon and implies wiping the slate clean.

Derivative: الرَّفَثُ refers to the intimacy between a husband and wife (الجِماع – Al-Jimaa’) and implies that which one is shy to speak about or mention i.e., it is an implicit way to refer to the intimacy between the husband and wife.

- “What Allah has written (مَا كَتَبَ اللَّهُ) for you (لَكُمْ)” refers to that which Allah has prescribed to be permissible for you and refers to something that is given as it is لَكُمْ (for you) like a dispensation. It differs from مَا كَتَبَ اللَّهُ عَلِيْكُم “What Allah has prescribed upon you” as the عَلِيْكُم (Upon you) implies that you have been tasked with something obligatory to undertake.

Derivative: تُدْلُوا comes from أَدْلَى – يُدْلِي – إِدْلَاء means to offer and present and is being used here in reference to payments made to people in positions of authority (الْحُكَّامِ – Al-Hukkaam) as bribes.

- “And approach (وَأْتُوا) the houses (الْبُيُوتَ) from (مِنْ) their doors (أَبْوَابِهَا)” – This follows the form of an Arabic expression which states: “approaching something from its doors” and implies that the matter which was approached was approached in the correct, proper and ethical manner i.e., the correct protocols were followed and no rules were broken or loop holes found.

Derivative: تَعْتَدُوا and the word الْمُعْتَدِينَ also mentioned in this verse comes from the verb اِعْتَدَى which means to aggress and also to cross or transgress against the limits. الْمُعْتَدِي (Al-Mu’tadi) is the aggressor or someone who transgresses against the limits. تَعْتَدُوا has been interpreted with the attached forbiddance of لا to mean the forbiddance of crossing the limits which Allah has set in respect to conduct in warfare and also with the meaning of it being forbidden to be the initial aggressors.

Derivative: الْفِتْنَةُ (Al-Fitnah) refers to trials and tests. It has been said that this refers to the trials and persecution that the Quraish of Makkah put the Muslims through. It has also been interpreted to refer to the dominance of Shirk and disbelief that takes people away from the Deen of Allah.
Derivative: الْفِتْنَةُ (Al-Fitnah) in general terms can refer to the situation where disobedience to Allah is easier and obedience to Him is harder, where disbelief is easier and Iman is harder.
Derivative: قِصَاصٌ (Qisaas) refers to equitable retribution and here it means to exact retribution for their aggression by fighting against them.

Derivative: الْحُرُمَاتُ (Al-Hurumaat) is the plural of حُرْمَة (Hurmah) and refers to that which is scared and inviolable i.e., can not be violated. It can refer to both places like Al-Masjid Al-Haraam and times like the sacred inviolable months.
- الشَّهْرُ الْحَرَامُ “Means the inviolable month” i.e., the month that is sacred during which it is impermissible to engage in fighting.

- “And do not throw [yourselves] (وَلَا تُلْقُوا) with your [own] hands (بِأَيْدِيكُمْ) into destruction (إِلَى التَّهْلُكَةِ)”. It is an expression that means “Don’t destroy or bring ruin to yourselves by your own hands” and is in reference to holding back from spending in Allah’s way.
Derivative: الْعِقَابِ (Al-‘Iqaab) refers to punishment and retribution and implies a bad outcome or consequence for a bad deed.
Derivative: فَرَضَ here implies the meaning of أَوْجَبَ عَلَى نَفْسِهِ, the one “who has made obligatory upon himself”.

Derivative: تَزَوَّدُوا is from زَوَّدَ which means to supply or equip with provisions. الزَّادِ (Az-Zaad) is that provision that you equip yourself with. It refers to what you pack for yourself to fulfil your requirements when travelling to a level of sufficiency that suffices your needs.
“And what (وَمَا) you do (تَفْعَلُوا) of good (مِنْ خَيْرٍ)” – The use of مَنْ (of/from) in conjunction with خَيْرٍ (good) implies “any good at all” that you do.
- Those of (أُولِي) pure minds (الْأَلْبَابِ)” – أَلْبَابِ (Albaab) is the plural of لُبٌّ (Lubb) which refers to a mind that is not contaminated and is capable of reasoning.

Derivative: أَفَضْتُم comes from أَفَاضَ which means to overflow and pour forth. It carries the meaning of being pushed forth in great quantities or large numbers. It is used here to describe the departing of the people on mass from Arafat.

- الْمَشْعَرِ الْحَرَامِ refers to Al-Muzdalifah. الْمَشْعَرِ (Al-Mash’ar) comes either from شُعور which reflects the meaning of ‘Ilm (knowledge and cognizance) or from شِعار (Shi’aar) which refers to a symbol and distinguishing mark. It is described as الْحَرَامِ (Al-Haraam - Sacred) due to it being in a sacred place and part of the rituals of Hajj.

Derivative: ذَٰلِكَ (Dhaalika) – It is often translated as “This” however “This” is هَذَا (Haadhaa) in the Arabic language. ذَٰلِكَ (Dhaalika) is used in Arabic for that which is far (بَعِيد – Ba’eed) whilst هَذَا (Haadhaa) is used for that which is close. This can imply to the object which is الْكِتَابُ (Al-Kitaab) being far away in Al-Lawh ul-Mahfouzh (preserved tablet). ذَٰلِكَ (Dhaalika) can also be used in the Arabic language in place of هَذَا (Haadhaa) to emphasise the standing of something. It has also been said that هَذَا (Haadhaa) and ذَٰلِكَ (Dhaalika) are interchangeable in the Arabic language and as such ذَٰلِكَ (Dhaalika) here is in the meaning of هَذَا (Haadhaa) “This”.
Derivative: هُدًى (Hudan) is commonly translated as "a guidance" but is also used in Arabic to emphasise the greatness. It thus implies the meaning of “an enormous or incredible guidance”.

Derivative: الْمُتَّقِينَ (Al-Muttaqeen) refers to those who have Taqwa (تَقْوَى) which implies more than the meaning of fear but rather the meaning of taking precaution and seeking to protect oneself.

- The form لَا رَيْبَ (Laa Raiba) “There is no doubt” is known as نَافِيَةٌ لِلْجِنْسِ (Naafiyah Lil-Jins) which is formed by placing a فَتْحَة (Fat’hah) at the end of a noun which is preceded by لَا (Laa) which negates the possibility of any exceptions to that noun. لَا رَيْبَ ۛ فِيهِ (Laa Raiba Feehi) therefore implies the meaning of “There’s no room whatsoever for any doubt in it”.

Derivative: تُحْشَرُونَ is from حَشَرَ which means to gather. تُحْشَرُونَ is in the passive voice plural and means “You will be gathered”. حَشَرَ is used in Arabic to refer to herding cattle together and as such implies a forced and involuntary gathering.
Derivative: أَلَدُّ comes from لَدَّ – لَدٌّ which refers to be vehement in quarrelling and opposition. أَلَدُّ (Aladdu) means the most vehement in his quarrelling and opposition.

Derivative: الْمِهَادُ refers to a resting place and being settled. It is used to refer to the bosom of the mother which she cradles and hugs her baby in. It provides the picture of hellfire being a place that clings on to and hugs its inhabitants.

Derivative: الْعِزَّةُ – The word عِزَّة (‘Izzah) combines the meanings of respect and power in Arabic. عَزيز (‘Azeez) is the one who commands respect because of his power. الْعِزَّةُ (Al-‘Izzah) here implies “pride” based on what he believes his status demands in terms of respect and being honoured. It refers to that inner pride that prevents the one possessing it from accepting advice.
Derivative: السِّلْمِ (As- Silm) can carry the meanings of peace, safety, surrender and submission. It is used to refer to Islaam.

Derivative: كَافَّةً (Kaaffah) implies the meanings of “completely”, “fully” and “entirely”. The مّدّ (Madd – Prolongation) of the أَلِف (Alif) in كَآفَّةً which is pronounced “Kaaaaaaffah” in accordance with the rules of recitation also adds emphasis to the word in this statement according to the precedent of increasing the word to increase its meaning.

- “O you who have believed” is used in the Qur’an in a broad manner to encompass a wide range of Muslims. In general, there can be said to be three categories of people. There is the category of the strongest believers or “Mu’minoon”, the category of the Muslim who has yet to taste real Iman, and even the hypocrites (Munaafiqoon) have been addressed by “O you who have believed” in the Qur’an. It is used to address the Muslim community as a whole.
Derivative: تَتَّقُونَ is from اِتَّقَى and it means to protect yourselves and to take precaution.

Derivative: يَأْتِيَ means to come but is also carries the meaning of coming upon something with the implication of attacking it and finishing it off. In English the statement: “I’m coming for you” carries such an implication
Derivative: يَنظُرُونَ from نَظَرَ which means to look and also to wait. The waiting implies procrastinating and putting things off.

Derivative: الدُّنْيَا (Ad-Dunyaa) refers to the worldly life. It is from the verb دَنَا which means to be close and lower. الدُّنْيَا (Ad-Dunyaa) implies the meaning of being close and inferior. It contrasts with عَالَم (‘Aalam) which describes the world from the perspective of what it is and contains and is the same for everyone. الدُّنْيَا (Ad-Dunyaa) in addition to being a general description of the life of this world can also imply a personal description of someone’s Dunyaa in the sense that everyone has a different worldly life. The life of the Dunyaa mentioned here refers to the material aspects of life and its pursuits which have become beautified to the disbelievers.
Derivative: بَغْيًا refers to the desire to become dominant and in control over others.
Derivative: لِ in لِيَحْكُمَ is called لام التَّعْلِيل (The Laam of giving reasoning) and is used to explain the reason for something.

- بِالْحَقِّ can mean “in truth” i.e., He sent with them (the Prophets) Al-Kitaab Bil-Haqq “In truth”. It can also mean with the truth i.e., containing the truth. And it can also imply “with purpose” i.e., Al-Kitaab was sent with a clear cause and purpose. الحَقّ (Al-Haqq) is also related to الحَقِيقَة (Al-Haqeeqah) which means “reality”, implying that the Book was sent so that it been seen in reality and in practise through its application and implementation.

- “In respect to whatever (فِيمَا) they differed in (اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ)” – The use of مَا (Maa) in فِيمَا (Feemaa – in what) indicates generality and encompasses “everything” that they differed upon or disagreed about i.e., in relation to all life’s affairs.
Derivative: خَيْرٍ (Khair) means “Good or goodness” which can refer here to a “good Halaal source” or “Good intent”, just خَيْرٍ (Khair) can refer to money here.

- “And Allah (وَاللَّهُ) knows (يَعْلَمُ) and/while you (وَأَنتُمْ) do not know (لَا تَعْلَمُونَ)” – The usual sentence structure is to use a verbal sentence with the verb beginning the sentence i.e., يَعْلَمُ اللَّهُ. This emphasises that Allah “knows” and implies: “It is Allah in fact who knows”. Even before it is stated: “And you do not know” this meaning has already been implied by the statement: وَاللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ “And it is Allah who knows” i.e., He alone knows.

Derivative: الْفِتْنَةُ refers to a trial and test. It also implies being seduced and tempted away from something. These meanings combine to provide the meaning of a trial that can tempt and take you away from guidance into misguidance i.e., that which leads to disbelief.

Derivative: حَبِطَتْ from حَبِطَ means to come to nothing and of no worth and to fail to see the fruits of one’s efforts i.e., the actions undertaken in the Dunyaa will bring no benefit and will become invalidated or null and void.

يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الشَّهْرِ الْحَرَامِ قِتَالٍ فِيهِ

- “They ask you concerning the sacred month, fighting in it” It is being addressed as a response to a military expedition prior to the battle of Badr led by Abdullah bin Jahsh (ra) in which one of Quraish was killed and the Muslims were accused of breaking the law by fighting in the sacred month of Rajab.
- الشَّهْرِ الْحَرَامِ “The scared or inviolable month” which refers to the sacred months of Dhu l-Qa’dah, Dhu l-Hijjah, Al-Muharram and Rajab in which the Arabs had prohibited fighting.
Derivative: جَاهَدُوا is from جَاهَدَ – جِهَاد and it literally means “to struggle”. In this context however the meaning of struggle is synonymous with the meaning of قِتال (Qitaal) fighting.

Derivative: يَرْجُونَ is from رَجَاء (Rajaa’) and means to have hope and in origin in Arabic in carries the meaning of expectation. يَرْجُونَ refers to those people who have expectation and has been interpreted by some to mean here that they deserve to have hope.

Derivative: هَاجَرُوا is from هَاجَرَ which means to undertake a هِجْرَة (Hijrah). Hijrah is used here to refer to migration and specifically the Hijrah undertaken by the Messenger ﷺ and his companions from Makkah to Al-Madinah. Hijrah literally means to abandon and separate or dissociate oneself from something. In Islam it can refer to leaving and separate yourself from anything that is disobedience to Allah for that which is obedience to Him.

- “No doubt, those who have believed and (وَ) those who have made Hijrah and undertaken Jihad in the way of Allah” – The واو (Waw – وَ) before those who made Hijrah is وَاوُ البَيَان (Waw ul-Bayaan) which is the Waw of elaboration. The meaning is therefore “No doubt, those who have believed, that is to say (وَ) those who have made Hijrah and undertaken Jihad in the way of Allah” in the case where those who made Hijrah and undertaken Jihad in the way of Allah is an elaboration of those who have believed and can be seen as a validation of their Iman (belief).
details.arabic_part: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَاجَرُوا وَجَاهَدُوا فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ

details.text: Derivative: الْخَمْرِ comes from the root of خ م ر which is the same root for the word خِمَار (Khimaar) which refers to a covering. خَمْر (Khamr – Wine/alcohol) comes from the same origin to refer to something that covers or overshadows your mind i.e., something that intoxicates and clouds the mind.

Derivative: الْعَفْوَ can mean “pardoning” i.e., when you let someone off or spare them a punishment. It can also be used in Arabic to refer to “giving something up”. الْعَفْوَ (Al-‘Afw) has been understood here in respect to spending, that you should spend from that which is in excess of your needs.
Derivative: مَنَافِعُ is the plural of مَنْفَع (Manfa’) which means “a benefit” i.e., something that can bring a benefit or be profited from.
- إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ means a major or great sin.

“And their sin (وَإِثْمُهُمَا) is greater (أَكْبَرُ) than their benefit (نَّفْعِهِمَا)” – Here it is important to note that Allah did not say: “Their harm (ضَارّ – Daarr)) is greater than the benefit that can be attained from them” but rather “their sin and evil (إِثْم - Ithm)” is greater than any benefit that can be gained from them.

Derivative: جَعَلَ when followed by a لَام (Laam – لِ) implies to provide and grant something as a favour. It can also mean to attribute something to something which is closer to its meaning used at the end of this Aayah in the word تَجْعَلُوا “They attribute”.

Derivative: فِرَاشًا comes from the word فَرَشَ which means to lay down and spread out. فِرَاشًا refers to something that is laid down and spread out to provide comfort and ease. It is used to refer to a bed, bedding and cushions.
Derivative: أَندَادًا is the plural of نِدٌّ which refers to an equal and rival.
Derivative: تُخَالِطُ is from خَالَطَ and means to mix (with).

- فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ “In respect to the life of this world and the next life” is the completion of the statement in the previous Aayah related to spending and the statement: كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمُ الْآيَاتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَفَكَّرُونَ “And in that way Allah makes clear for you the Aayaat so that you may thing deeply” i.e., “So that you may thing deeply in respect to the life of this world and the next life”.

Derivative: تُنكِحُوا is from أَنْكَحَ (Ankaha) and it means “to get someone (else) married” and differs from نَكَحَ (Nakaha) which means “to marry”. The beginning of this verse states لَا تَنكِحُوا الْمُشْرِكَاتِ “Don’t marry (لَا تَنكِحُوا) the female idol worshippers (الْمُشْرِكَاتِ)” which is from the verb نَكَحَ (Nakaha) and then after that it states: وَلَا تُنكِحُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ “And don’t marry someone else (لَا تُنكِحُوا) to the male idol worshippers (الْمُشْرِكِينَ)” which is from the verb أَنْكَحَ (Ankaha). In both instances the command to not do something is addressed to you in the male plural form أَنْتُم (Antum – You m.pl) - لَا تَنكِحُوا “Don’t marry” and لَا تُنكِحُوا “Don’t marry somebody else” i.e., don’t marry your women in the case where “women” is not explicitly mentioned but implicitly understood. This means that the men responsible for the women (i.e., their Walis) were addressed alone with the command and not the women themselves.
Derivative: تَطَهَّرْنَ is from تَطَهَّرَ which means to make the effort to purify oneself and implies the Ghusl (full washing from impurity).

Derivative: أَذًى (Adhan) – Here the Masdar is used instead of the descriptive word and as such أَذًى (Adhan) implies the meaning of “pain or harm or source of suffering” itself instead of the meaning of “harmful or painful”. The Masdar reflects the conception and idea of something and is used as a form of hyperbole for the purpose of emphasis.

Derivative: الْمَحِيضِ is a ظَرْف (Zharf – Indicator to time or place) upon the وَزْن (Wazn – pattern) of مَفْعِل (Maf’il) from the word حَيْض (Haid) which means menstruation. مَحِيضِ (Maheed) refers to the time of menstruation and the woman’s impurity.
Derivative: اعْتَزِلُوا is the plural command form from اِعْتَزَلَ which means to stay away and avoid someone or something i.e., to isolate and withdraw.

Derivative: يَطْهُرْنَ “They (female plural) become pure” – It comes from طَهُرَ which is a form (فَعُلَ – Fa’ula) that implies for something to become something. It implies that the women are inherently pure and are only impure temporarily until they return to their state of purity.

وَيَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْمَحِيضِ ۖ قُلْ هُوَ أَذًى فَاعْتَزِلُوا النِّسَاءَ فِي الْمَحِيضِ ۖ وَلَا تَقْرَبُوهُنَّ حَتَّىٰ يَطْهُرْنَ ۖ فَإِذَا تَطَهَّرْنَ فَأْتُوهُنَّ مِنْ حَيْثُ أَمَرَكُمُ اللَّهُ

- There is an insight in this verse that applies to other similar verses in the Qur’an which discuss intimacy between the husband and wife. It uses language that is implicit and not explicit. وَيَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْمَحِيضِ “They ask you about the time of menstruation” is stated without stating what is explicitly the question. وَلَا تَقْرَبُوهُنَّ “And don’t draw near to them” and فَأْتُوهُنَّ “Approach them” are used instead of explicit terms.

“No doubt (or surely), Allah loves those who are constantly repenting (التَّوَّابِينَ) and those who purify themselves (الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ)” – التَّوَّابِينَ (At-Tawwaabeen) are those who spiritually cleanse themselves and الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ (Al-Mutatahhireen) are those who physically purify themselves. Both are in the male plural form which indicates that it covers both the men and women even though it is the women that are addressed in this verse. That indicates that these rulings and consequences of them are applicable to both. The word يُحِبُّ (He loves) is repeated for both التَّوَّابِينَ (At-Tawwaabeen) and الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ (Al-Mutatahhireen) to highlight that each of these characteristics are valued highly in their own right.
details.arabic_part: إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ التَّوَّابِينَ وَيُحِبُّ الْمُتَطَهِّرِينَ ‎

details.text: Derivative: حَرْثٌ means a place for planting and is used to refer to the ground or soil in which the farmer plants his seeds.

Derivative: مُّلَاقُو is from the verb لَاقَى means “to meet” and implies that one of the parties made the effort to meet the other as opposed to the verb تَلَاقَى which means that the parties meeting met at the same time in an equal manner. مُّلَاقُو therefore implies that it is you who will go to meet Allah. The use of مُّلَاقُو which is a noun instead of the verb which dictates being attached directly to the object of the meeting مُّلَاقُوهُ “You will meet Him” can imply closeness between the one meeting and the one he is meeting and that nothing will come between that meeting.

“And send forward (وَقَدِّمُوا) for yourselves (لِأَنفُسِكُمْ)” means to engage in good deeds in your present as an investment for your future. In this context of marital relations, those good deeds related to marriage and having children can be implied here.
Derivative: عُرْضَةً means as an obstacle or prevention and here it implies “as a reason or pretext to prevent” doing something.
Derivative: تَتَّقُوا means to protect yourself from acts of disobedience to Allah.

Derivative: أَيْمَانِ (Aymaan) is the plural of يَمِين (Yameen) which means “right i.e., direction or side)”, the “right hand” and also an “oath” that is sworn. The connection is that the “oath” was undertaken by the shaking of the “right hands”.
Derivative: تَبَرُّوا means to do good, act righteously and undertake acts of obedience to Allah. It can imply to do good for others.

- تُصْلِحُوا comes from the verb أَصِلَحَ which means “to make right, to rectify and mend”. The phrase تُصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ النَّاسِ means “making peace and reconciliation between the people” i.e., mending the problem or disagreement between them.
Derivative: يُؤَاخِذُ literally means “to take hold of” and is used to mean “to take to task” or “hold to account”.
Derivative: اللَّغْوِ (Al-Laghw) refers to “empty or aimless speech” i.e., something that is said casually without thought.
- It literally means “By what (بِمَا) your hearts (كَسَبَتْ) earned (قُلُوبُكُمْ)” and is an expression that means “What you intended, meant and was intentional”.

Derivative: يُؤْلُونَ comes from إِيلَاء which literally means “to make an oath” and here it carries the specific meaning of someone swearing an oath that he will not be intimate with his wife or share the same bed as her.
Derivative: فَاءُوا comes from فَاءَ which means to return and is used when somebody returns after making a mistake.
Derivative: الطَّلَاقَ (At-Talaaq) emanates from the meaning of أَطْلَقَ which means to release something or to let it go. It is the word used to mean “divorce”.
Derivative: عَزَمُوا is from عَزَمَ which means “to have made a firm decision” or “to be determined and resolved” to do something.

Derivative: حَكِيمٌ (Hakeem) has two meanings that can be attached to it. The first is حِكْمَة (Hikmah – Wisdom) and the second is حُكْم (Hukm – Judgement). From Hikmah Hakeem can imply that Allah’s wisdom is constant and from Hukm Hakeem can be understood to mean that Allah’s judgment and verdicts are absolute.

Derivative: عَزِيزٌ is often translated as “All-Mighty”. However, عَزِيزٌ (‘Azeez) combines two matters, قُوَّة (Quwwah - Power) and كَرَامَة (Karaamah – Standing of respect and honour). عَزِيزٌ (‘Azeez) therefore combines that which has power and authority and that which commands respect.

- “And they (the wives) have to them (لَهُنَّ) like what they have due upon them (عَلَيْهِنَّ), according to what is Ma’ruf” – The use of لَهُنَّ “To them” and عَلَيْهِنَّ “Upon them” is a manner of saying “Their rights” and “Their obligations”, in the case where لِ indicates to something being due “to” somebody (i.e., a right) and عَلَى indicates to something being “due upon” somebody (i.e., an obligation or responsibility).
- بِالْمَعْرُوفِ (Bil-Ma’ruf) implies “In a manner that is widely known, customary and regarded to be fair in its circumstances”.

Derivative: إِحْسَانٍ (Ihsaan) means in the best of ways. Ihsaan in relation to others means to treat them in the best manner and to be good to them so that all rights are fulfilled towards them and they are not wronged in any way.

Derivative: يَتَعَدَّ is from تَعَدَّى which means to cross, overstep, to exceed or go beyond. It is used with حُدُودَ اللَّهِ “The limits or boundaries of Allah” and conjures the image of their being a boundary that we are forbidden to cross its line.

Derivative: مَعْرُوفٍ (Ma’roof) is an اِسْم المَفْعول (Ism ul-Maf’ool) and its literal translation is that which is “known or recognised”. It implies that which is recognised by society to be the customary and decent thing to do i.e., in accordance with the customary standards and societal norms. In respect to taking one’s wife back it implies to take her back in a manner where you will live with her in accordance with the correct manners and standards of living with a wife and being a companion to her.
- “The limits (حُدُودَ) of Allah (اللَّهِ)” – These limits or boundaries (Hudood)) refers to the rulings which Allah has set and ordained.

Derivative: شُهَدَاءَ means witnesses and it can imply those experts who bear witness to attest to what you bring. It can imply the false deities that they worship and it can also implies supporters to assist in the effort.

Derivative: دُونِ carries the meaning of غَيْر (Ghair) which means “other (than)” or “besides”. دُونِ (Doon) also implies in its meaning being less than and inferior to, which in this context can add the implication of “whoever you call besides Allah will inevitably be inferior”.

Derivative: إِن (In) means “if” and is used when the expectation is less than if إِذَا (Idhaa) is used which can mean “when” and “if” but when used with the meaning of “If” the expectation of its occurrence is higher. There’s also a pessimism implied with إِن (In) whilst an optimism is implied with إِذَا (Idhaa).

“To a people (لِقَوْمٍ) that wants to know (يَعْلَمُونَ)” – The use of the present tense verb يَعْلَمُونَ implies an effort to accomplish the verb i.e., a people who want to know or wants to make effort to know. If the noun was used, then it would suggest that “they know” and already have the knowledge.
Derivative: تَعْضُلُوا comes from عَضَلَ which means to prevent and is used specifically in relation to preventing a woman from marrying someone.

Derivative: مَتِّعُوا is from مَتَّعَ which means to give a provision that benefits the person. In this context it refers to some kind of compensation that the man gives to a woman when he divorces her before consummation and before setting the dowry so that she may benefit from it.
Derivative: الْمُقْتِرِ (Al-Muqtar) is the person who lives in constrained circumstances i.e., is poor.
Derivative: الْمُوسِعِ (Al-Moosi’) is the person who lives in unconstrained circumstances i.e., he is rich and wealthy.
Derivative: الْحِجَارَةُ means stones and can refer to the idols which were made from stone or to imply the intensity of the heat by stating that even stones will be fuel for the fire.

Derivative: كَافِرِينَ (Kaafireen) refers to disbelievers and comes from the verb كَفَرَ which was used by the Arabs for the action of burying a seed in the ground. The broken plural of كَافِر (Kaafir) is كُفَّار (Kuffar) which was used to refer to farmers. The implication is that the disbeliever already had the seed of truth within him when he came into this earth and then buried that seed (i.e., the truth) deep inside himself and didn’t let it see the light of day.

Derivative: عَزِيزٌ is often translated as “All-Mighty”. However, عَزِيزٌ (‘Azeez) combines two matters, قُوَّة (Quwwah - Power) and كَرَامَة (Karaamah – Standing of respect and honour). عَزِيزٌ (‘Azeez) therefore combines that which has power and authority and that which commands respect.

Derivative: حَكِيمٌ (Hakeem) has two meanings that can be attached to it. The first is حِكْمَة (Hikmah – Wisdom) and the second is حُكْم (Hukm – Judgement). From Hikmah Hakeem can imply that Allah’s wisdom is constant and from Hukm Hakeem can be understood to mean that Allah’s judgment and verdicts are absolute.

Derivative: مَتَاعٌ refers to a provision that is benefited from and utility is gained from it. Here it refers to what is known as “maintenance” which those responsible provide to a person who has a right to it.
Derivative: يُقْرِضُ is from أَقْرَضَ which means to give a loan. It is one of the words used in the Qur’an for spending for the sake of Allah. The use of أَقْرَضَ implies that it will be repaid.
Derivative: عَسَيْتُمْ comes from عَسَى is usually attached to an action to imply that something is probable, likely or might well occur e.g., “It may be that …”, “It could possibly be that …”.

Derivative: الْمَلَإِ comes from the verb مَلَأ (Mala’) which means “to fill”. الْمَلَإِ is used to describe the chiefs of a time i.e., leaders and people of influence. The connection to the verb “to fill” is that these people (الْمَلَإِ) fill the king’s court. It refers to those who have access to that which normal people don’t have access to and have influence in matters related to the running of affairs.

- “Have you not (أَلَمْ) looked (تَرَ) to or at?” – The verb تَرَ is from رَأَى which means “to see”. However, the “seeing” can be literal just as it can be figurative implying that something is cognitively understood. Its figurative meaning here can be understood to imply “to consider” i.e., “Have you not considered?”.

“And Allah is ‘Aleem (وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ) with those who do wrong (بِالظَّالِمِينَ)” – The use of عَلِيمٌ (‘Aleem – Fully knowledgeable and aware) implies that Allah is always aware of the wrongdoers, whether they are from Bani Isra’eel like in this Aayah or at the time of the Messenger ﷺ and the hypocrites or in our time.
Derivative: قَدْ (Qad) is used for the purpose of emphasis and with the past tense it indicates to certainty.

Derivative: أَنَّىٰ carries the meaning of “How” like كَيْفَ (Kaifa) however it is said when something is not understood and appears to be inexplicable, like something involving shock or surprise. Its meaning is therefore close to “How can it be that?!”

Derivative: سَعَةً (Sa’ah) comes from وَسَعٌ (Was’) which expanse and vastness. It is the opposite of ضَيِّق (Dayyiq) which means constraint. سَعَةً (Sa’ah) means abundance and plenty implying affluence and wealth.

Derivative: اصْطَفَى comes from صَفْوَة (Safwah) which means “pure” and “clear” i.e., unblemished. اصْطَفَى (Istafaa) means “to choose or select” and is used when the choice is pure, when the choice is based on merit. اصْطَفَى (Istafaa) also carries the implication that the choice that is made is solely the selector’s choice and not influenced by others and requires no explanation or justification.
Derivative: بَسْطَةً means “expanse”, “abundance” and “scope in ability and capability”.

Derivative: إِنَّ (Inna) is used in Arabic for the purpose of removing the doubt (إِزَالَةُ الشَّكّ – Izaalat ush-Shakk) and carries the meaning of “No doubt” or “For sure” etc. It is therefore used for emphasis.

- “And their Prophet (نَبِيُّهُمْ) said to them (قَالَ لَهُمْ)” – The mention of نَبِيُّهُمْ “Their Prophet” is to draw extra emphasis to the Prophet here and to the fact that it is “their” Prophet who is addressing them. The fact that here it states: “Their Prophet” (نَبِيُّهُمْ) instead of: “A Prophet of theirs” (لِنَبِيٍّ لَّهُمُ) as was stated in the previous verse, indicates that after their request to him, he now has a stronger connection to them.

- “Indeed (إِنَّ), Allah (اللَّهَ) has appointed (قَدْ بَعَثَ) for you (لَكُمْ) Talut (طَالُوتَ) as a king (مَلِكًا)” – It is observed that the sentence structure has been adjusted from its usual order by bringing لَكُمْ “For you” earlier in the sentence order. This draws attention to the fact that the appointment from Allah for Talut to be king was made especially for you. It can imply that this appointment was made for your own benefit and good. The use of إِنَّ and قَدْ also place a lot of emphasise on the fact that it was Allah who appointed Talut.
details.arabic_part: إِنَّ اللَّهَ قَدْ بَعَثَ لَكُمْ طَالُوتَ مَلِكًا

details.text: Derivative: يَأْتِيَ from أَتَى means “to come” and can carry the implication of something that comes smoothly, without difficulty and struggle.

Derivative: التَّابُوتُ (At-Taaboot) refers to the “chest” i.e., box or container. It was said to contain the Tawrah and other remnants or artefacts from the families (or lines) of Musa (as) and Harun (as) including Musa’s staff, remnants of the tablets and other items. The chest had been lost to the children of Isra’eel and taken from them prior to that.
“If (إِن) you are (كُنتُم) believers (مُّؤْمِنِينَ)” – The use of إِن (In – If) implies that there is a doubt in respect to them actually being believers.

Derivative: الصَّابِرِينَ (As-Saabireen) is from صَبْر (Sabr) and means those who have Sabr. Sabr implies to maintain one’s course and path regardless of the circumstances one is faced with i.e., to persevere and remain steadfast. It is used frequently in reference to those who fight in the way of Allah and those who are being tested in this life.

Derivative: مُّلَاقُو is the plural اِسْمُ الفَاعِل (Ism ul-Maf’ool – Active participle) form from the verb لَاقَى – يُلَاقِي – مُلَاقاة which means to meet and is stronger and more powerful in terms of the impact of the meeting than the verb لَقِيَ which just means to meet.
Derivative: فِئَةٍ (Fi’ah) implies a small group of people as opposed to طَائِفَة which implies a larger collective group.
Derivative: جَاوَزَ means to “traverse, pass or cross” something like an obstacle or border or in this case a river. It can also mean to pass a test or obstacle in terms of getting past it
Derivative: يَطْعَمْ comes from طَعَمَ – طَعْم and means to taste and can imply a slight tasting of something.

Derivative: مُبْتَلِي comes from the verb اِبْتَلَى (Ibtalaa) which means to “test” or “put to trial” and اِبْتِلاء (Ibtilaa’ – Test/trial) is stronger than بَلاء (Balaa’) in Arabic i.e., a more testing trial or severer test.
Derivative: فَصَلَ (Fasala) implies two meanings: To be cut off and to be far i.e., that Talut and his soldiers had completely left their base and were far removed from their homes and towns.
Derivative: لَيْسَ مِنِّي literally means “He is not from me” and is an Arabic expression to imply dissociation and means that He has no connection or relationship with me.

- “There is no capacity (capability or power) (لَا طَاقَةَ) for us (لَنَا)” – The construct لَا طَاقَةَ is called لَا نَافِيَة لِلْجِنْس (Laa Naafiyah Lil-Jins – The Laa of negating the category of something) and is distinguished by the word following the negation of لَا being in the Mansoob (accusative) case form with no Tanween: لَا طَاقَةَ (Laa Taaqata). It implies the absolute and categorical negation of something that “We have no power or capability at all”.
“HOW MANY (كَم مِّن) small groups (فِئَةٍ قَلِيلَةٍ)” – The مِّن added to the كَمْ (How many) adds emphasis to the “how many” and implies that “there have been many small groups”.

- The phrase الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (Alladheena Aamanoo) “Those who have believed” can encompass within its scope those who have strong belief, those with weaker belief and also those who declare belief falsely i.e., hypocrites. However, here it is qualified by what is mentioned in the remainder of the Aayah and refers to the true believers.

Derivative: أَفْرِغْ is the command form of أَفْرَغَ (Afragha). فَرَاغ (Faraagh) in Arabic means emptiness. أَفْرَغَ (Afragha) means to empty something out. When used with عَلَى (on or upon) it means to empty something out on top of something else. It therefore implies emptying and pouring something from its source to the vessel that receives it. In the context here it is a Du’aa for Allah to pour upon us Sabr (patience and steadfastness) so that we are saturated by it: رَبَّنَا أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنَا صَبْرًا “O our Lord, pour upon us Sabr”.

Derivative: جُنُودِ (Junood) can be the plural of جُنْد (Jund) which refers to an army or army regiment. It can also be the plural of جُنْدِي (Jundiy) which is a soldier. As such جُنُودِ (Junood) can refer top hosts of armies or regiments, or it can refer to soldiers.
Derivative: صَبْرًا (Sabr) implies to maintain one’s commitment and to remain consistent and on course.

Derivative: انصُرْنَا is from نَصَرَ – نَصْر. نَصْر (Nasr) is one of the words used for aid and assistance. It is usually used for major forms of assistance like against enemies on the battlefield or oppressors.
Derivative: بَرَزُوا means to come to appear in full view in front of something. In refers here to the army of Talut being face to face with the army of Jalut.
Derivative: دَفْعُ (Daf’) from دَفَعَ means “to push” or “to repel”.
Derivative: الْحِكْمَةَ (Al-Hikmah) refers to wisdom and it has been said to refer here to Prophethood.

Derivative: فَ – The فاء (Faa’ – فَ) here is فَاءُ السَّبَبِيَة (Faa’u s-Sababiyah – The Faa’ of causality) and implies the meaning of “As such” or “therefore” or “As a result of that”. It connects what follows it with what preceded it i.e., as a result of the Du’aa they made, they defeated their enemy by Allah’s permission. This فاء (Faa’ – فَ) often comes in the Qur’an following a Du’aa (supplication) highlighting the connection between the Du’aa and its result. فاء (Faa’ – فَ) also implies immediacy in terms of the result happening quickly.
Derivative: هَزَمُوا is from هَزَمَ which means “to defeat” someone but implies the absolute defeating where the enemy was vanquished and crushed.

“And had it not been for the Daf’ (دَفْعُ) of Allah of the people, some of them by others …” – The meaning of دَفْعُ (Daf’) here can mean “to push” with the meaning of “Allah replacing (i.e., pushing aside) the people, some of them by others” i.e., Allah replace one nation, power, dynasty or rule with another. it can also carry the meaning “to push” with the meaning of repelling, pushing back and driving off, with the meaning of “Allah repelling the people, some of them by others” i.e., the people of truth (Al-Haqq) repelling and countering the people of falsehood (Al-Baatil).
details.arabic_part: وَلَوْلَا دَفْعُ اللَّهِ النَّاسَ بَعْضَهُم بِبَعْضٍ

details.text: Derivative: فَضَّلْنَا “We gave preference or favoured” comes from the word فَضَّلَ – تَفْضِيل which means to “give preference or to favour”. تَفْضِيل (Tafdeel) differs from تَفْرِيق (Tafreeq) in respect to the Messengers and Prophets of Allah, in that تَفْرِيق (Tafreeq) means to distinguish between them in terms of belief (i.e., I believe in some but not all) whilst تَفْضِيل (Tafdeel) means that some have been favoured or have been given preference by Allah over others.
Derivative: اقْتَتَلَ means to fight each other i.e., infighting amongst themselves.
