- “A day (يَوْمٌ) in which (فِيهِ) there will be no trading (لَّا بَيْعٌ) in it and no friendship (وَلَا خُلَّةٌ) and no intercession (وَلَا شَفَاعَةٌ)” – It is observable that the لَا نَافِيَة الجِنْس (Laa Naafiyat ul-Jins) form which negates the object being negated absolutely was not used here and this implies that these negations of there being no trading, friendship nor intercession on that day are general negations with the possibility remaining for there to be exceptions to that.
details.arabic_part: يَوْمٌ لَّا بَيْعٌ فِيهِ وَلَا خُلَّةٌ وَلَا شَفَاعَةٌ

details.text: Derivative: إِلَٰهَ (Ilaah) can hold several meanings in the Arabic language. The verb أَلِهَ – يَأْلَهُ can mean to have extreme love for something and it can mean to lean on something or someone. إِلَٰهَ (Ilaah) can also refer to an authority that demands obedience. In sum إِلَٰهَ (Ilaah) contains the meaning of the entity that is worshipped, obeyed and loved.
Derivative: يَئُودُ is from آدَ and means to guard something or undertake a matter for a long period of time and to get tired from that and implies to burden or toil or bring difficulty and hardship.

Derivative: مَا is a term that implies generality and is more comprehensive implying the meaning of “Whatever” i.e., everything. مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ “Whatever (مَا) is in the heavens (فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ) and in the earth (وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ)”. مَنْ (Man) means “Whoever” and is used for humans or that which possesses an عَقْل (‘Aql – Mind) whilst مَا is more comprehensive and includes the meaning of “whoever” within the meaning of “whatever” i.e., all creation including humans, Angels, Jinn and non-Humans including all objects, things and animals etc.
Derivative: سِنَةٌ refers to that which precedes sleep in terms of tiredness and drowsiness.

Derivative: الْقَيُّوم (Al-Qayyoom) is a rare hyperbolised (مُبَالَغَة - Mubaalaghah) form of قَائِم (Qaa’im) and reflects the meaning of the One who is extremely and continually engaged in watching over things to make sure that they keep standing, are maintained and do not fail or break apart.

Derivative: الْحَيُّ (Al-Hayy) literally means “the living” and the addition of الْ (Alif Laam) denotes the absence of another i.e., that He alone is Al-Hayy. It has been understood to mean that He is the source of life and all life comes back to Him. It has also been understood to mean “The ever living” or “The One who is everlasting and always in existence” and “The One whose existence is not reliant on another”.
- لَا إِلَٰهَ (Laa Ilaaha) is in the لَا نَافِيَة الجِنْس (Laa Naafiyat ul-Jins – Negation of the category of the word) form which dictates the meaning there is absolutely no Ilaah (other than Him).

- مَن ذَا “Who is it who will” do such and such a thing. It is used like a challenge implying: “Who is it who will dare” to do such and such a thing. ذَا can also be the shortened or original form of هَذا (Haadha – This) with the meaning of “Who is this” i.e., who is this person or idol who thinks that he has some authority to intercede.

Derivative: إِكْرَاهَ (Ikraah) – أَكْرَهَ – يُكْرِهُ – إِكْرَاه means to force somebody to do something they hate or dislike i.e., against their will. إِكْرَاهَ (Ikraah) means compulsion and coercion.
Derivative: الْعُرْوَةِ (‘Urwah) refers to a “handhold” which is like a loop of tied rope that is used to place one’s hand in and grip to climb or support oneself.

Derivative: الطَّاغُوتِ is from طَغَى – يَطْغَي - طُغْيَان which means to rebel and transgress or overstep the proper bounds and to be excessive. الطَّاغُوتِ (Taaghoot)refers to any entity that rebels against Allah and causes or calls others to rebel and disobey Allah or and entity that maintains disbelief and oppression. It can include false gods, people in authority, institutions or any entity of authority and influence.
- لَا إِكْرَاهَ uses the لَا نَافِيَة الجِنْس (Laa Naafiyat ul-Jins – Negation of the category of the word) form and as such implies: “There is absolutely no compulsion or coercion at all”.

Derivative: وَلِيُّ (Waliy) means “Friend, protector or ally”. مَوْلَى (Mawlaa) is another word from the same root which is used in the Qur’an. There is a difference between “protecting” and being “protective”. وَلِيُّ (Waliy) refers to a “protective” friend whilst مَوْلَى (Mawlaa) refers to the one who is actively engaged in “protecting” someone. The Taaghoot are the Awliyaa’ (plural of Waliy) of the disbelievers but they do not have a Mawlaa (Surah Muhammad: 11).

- “He takes them out (يُخْرِجُهُم) from (مِّنَ) the darknesses (الظُّلُمَاتِ) to (إِلَى) the light (النُّورِ)” – The darknesses (الظُّلُمَاتِ – Azh-Zhulumaat) are plural and multiple whilst the light (النُّورِ – An-Noor) is singular. الظُّلُمَاتِ (Azh-Zhulumaat) refers to there being various kinds, flavours and sources of darkness (disbelief and misguidance) whilst there is only one Noor (light) i.e., Islam.
Derivative: نُنشِزُ is from the verb أَنْشَزَ – يُنْشِزُ – إِنْشَاز means to make rise up, to revitalise and bring to life.

- خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا literally means “to have fallen down upon its roofs” and is used as an expression to imply complete ruin and deterioration that comes to a place that has been abandoned for a long time by people and not maintained.

Derivative: مَثَلًا is used for the purpose of تَمْيِيز (Tamyeez) and as such means “as an example” or “for example” in the context of the statement: “What did Allah intend by this, as an example (مَثَلًا)”.

Derivative: فَمَا فَوْقَهَا – "Or (then) what is above it” – Here “above” can mean “larger” and it can also carry the meaning of “beyond that” with the implied meaning of “even smaller than it (i.e., a mosquito)”.

- To strike (يَضْرِبَ) an example (مَثَلًا) is an Arabic expression to “provide or offer and an example” and uses the word ضَرْب (strike) because an example is used to draw the attention of the audience and it can also imply that the example has an impact.

Derivative: سَنَابِلَ (Sanaabil) is the plural of سُنبُلَةٍ (Sunbalah) which is an ear of grain (i.e., the top part of the stem which is also known as the spike in cereal plants such as wheat and maize). سُنبُلَةٍ (Sunbalah) has a sound plural of سُنبُلَات (Sunbalaat) which is a جَمْع القِلَّة (Jam’u l-Qillah -A plural of a little) whilst its broken plural سَنَابِلَ (Sanaabil) is جَمْع الكَثْرَة (Jam’u l-Kathrah - A plural of a lot). It is a hyperbolised plural emphasising that it relates to something that is extremely abundant and keeps increasing. In Surah Yusuf when the context was one of a drought and there being shortages the use of the plural form سُنبُلَات (Sunbalaat) reflects that.

- فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ (Fee Sabeelillah) literally means “In the path of Allah”. In relation to spending, it has a specific meaning which differs from other types of spending and Sadaqaat. It refers to that which is related to fulfilling the mission of Islam to make it prevail and Al-Jihad has become closely attached to the expression.
Derivative: أَذًى means harm, pain and hurt. Here it refers to hurtful and degrading words in the case where your spending upon someone gives you an advantage to exploit that person to abuse him.

Derivative: مَنًّا refers here to seeking leverage from spending or giving where the giver imposes his will upon the one he has given to or uses his giving for advantage or vanity. It manifests in the person who has spent or given reminding the person whom he spent upon or gave to of the fact that he had given to him or spent upon him for the purpose of showing off in arrogance about it or to take advantage of his giving based on feeling a sense of entitlement.
Derivative: صَلْدًا means bare i.e., nothing remaining on it.
Derivative: صَفْوَانٍ refers to a smooth rocks or boulders.
Derivative: تَيَمَّمُوا from تَيَمَّمَ which means “to aim towards” something making it the target of what you are seeking.
- It literally means: “The Shaytan promises you poverty” however its meaning is that Shaytan makes you fearful of falling into poverty if you were to spend on others on in the way of Allah.

Derivative: الْحِكْمَةَ (Al-Hikmah - Wisdom) is when one understands the depths of things and that impacts one’s life. The Arabs considered Al-Hikmah to be beneficial knowledge that was acted upon and the person who possessed that is a حَكِيم (Hakeem).
- أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ (Uolu l-Albaab) is an expression to refer to those who possess pure and clear minds or sound intellects which are free of biases and whims.
Derivative: نَذَرْتُم is from نَذَرَ – نُذْر and means to make a vow or pledge. Here it refers to what “You have made vows upon” in terms of the undertaking of future good acts.
- فَنِعِمَّا هِيَ carries the meaning of “Then it is all good and well”.
Derivative: يُوَفَّ from وَفَّى – يُوَفِّي and it means to be given back or repaid in full. يُوَفَّ is in the passive voice and therefore means: “It will be given back in full (إِلَيْكُمْ - to you)”.

- “And you will not be spending (وَمَا تُنفِقُونَ) except (إِلَّا) seeking (ابْتِغَاءَ) the face/pleasure of Allah (وَجْهِ اللَّهِ)” – Here the مَا (Maa) is for the purpose of negation and this statement of fact “That you won’t be doing something” implies a command i.e., “Don’t spend except seeking the pleasure of Allah”. An alternative understanding of this statement is “You are not spending unless it is seeking the face or pleasure of Allah” i.e., if its not done seeking His face and pleasure, then it holds no value and is given no consideration.
details.arabic_part: وَمَا تُنفِقُونَ إِلَّا ابْتِغَاءَ وَجْهِ اللَّهِ

details.text: Derivative: أُحْصِرُوا comes from أَحْصَرَ which can mean to be constrained, confined or engulfed. Its meaning here can mean that they have been restricted by being engaged in the path of Allah (i.e., Al-Jihad and seeking knowledge) or that they have restricted themselves and dedicated themselves to the path of Allah.

Derivative: إِلْحَافًا (Ilhaaf) – لِحاف (Lihaaf) is a cover or blanket that someone wraps around himself. إِلْحَافًا (Ilhaaf) literally implies to be made to wrap around and is used in Arabic to refer to persistence and badgering.

Derivative: التَّعَفُّفِ (At-Ta’affuf) means “restraint and abstinence”. Here it is describing the condition of those Fuqaraa’ (poor) and that they do not ask others for help due to their integrity and modesty which may lead someone ignorant to believe that they are not in need of help.
- ضَرْبًا فِي الْأَرْضِ “Darb on or in the earth” – ضَرْبًا here means “to travel” and with “in the earth” it implies the meaning of “to travel to make a living”.

- لِلْفُقَرَاءِ (Li l-Fuqara’ – For the Poor) – This follows on from the previous verses discussing spending that is done for Allah’s pleasure. The beginning of the statement has been omitted but understood. Its meaning is therefore understood to be: “The Sadaqaat is for the Fuqaraa’ (poor)”.
- “You will know or recognise them (تَعْرِفُهُم) by their marks (بِسِيمَاهُمْ)” i.e., you will recognise the effects of poverty and being in need in them.

- “And it won’t be (وَلَا) them (هُمْ) who will be grieving (يَحْزَنُونَ)” – The addition of هُمْ (Hum – Them) and it being brought forward in the sentence structure before the verb implies: “It won’t be “them” who will be grieving” with the added implication of “rather it will be others”.

- This is an expression to mean “to be driven to insanity” and literally implies to be continuously beaten all over by Shaytan leaving him in a state of insanity. It conjures the image of someone in an extreme state of bewilderment and confusion.
Derivative: أَثِيمٍ (Atheem) is a hyperbolised form and means constantly sinful.

Derivative: يُرْبِي comes from أَرْبَى which means to make grow or to give increase. It comes from the same root as رِِبَا (Ribaa’) which comes from رَبَا – يَرْبُو - رَبْوًا which linguistically means to grow and increase.
Derivative: يَمْحَقُ is from the verb مَحَقَ which means to obliterate and eradicate.
Derivative: كَفَّارٍ (Kaffaar) is a hyperbolised form and means excessively ungrateful.

Derivative: تَكْفُرُونَ “You (plural) disbelieve” is from كُفْر (Kufr) which means to deny someone or something or to be ungrateful to someone or something, in the case where ingratitude is كُفْرَان بِالنِّعْمَة “The denial of the favour or blessing”.
Derivative: تُوَفَّىٰ means “to be given in full” i.e., the exact amount that is due.
Derivative: تَسْأَمُوا is from the verb سَئِمَ – يَسْأَمُ which means to get tired, fed up or weary of doing something. Its meaning here is to be lax in respect to undertaking the act.

Derivative: يُضَارَّ (Yudaarra) is from ضَارَّ which means to bring harm. The form يُضَارَّ (Yudaarra) here fits both the active and passive forms and as such can mean “to bring harm” or “to have harm brought upon”. In this verse it can therefore mean: “And a writer and a witness should not bring harm” or “And a writer and witness should not have harm brought to them”. The word يُضَارَّ can imply to being pressure to bear.
Derivative: رِهَانٌ (Rihaan) is the plural of رَهْن (Rahn) and refers to that which is left with someone as a security for the loan that they are giving.

- “Then no doubt (فَإِنَّهُ) his heart (قَلْبُهُ) is sinful (آثِمٌ)” – The word آثِمٌ (sinful) which is the Khabar (predicate) is brought forward in the sentence structure and placed before the Mubtada’ (subject) of قَلْبُهُ (his heart). This adds emphasis to the sinfulness of his heart with the meaning: “How incredibly sinful his heart is”.
Derivative: غُفْرَانَ is in the مُبَالَغَة (Mubaalaghah – Hyperbolised) form and carries the meaning of “Tremendous forgiveness”.

- غُفْرَانَكَ “Your forgiveness” – غُفْرَانَ “Tremendous forgiveness” is the object of the verb which has been omitted but implied. The verb carries the meaning of “We seek” or “We ask for” or “Grant us”. The meaning is therefore: “We ask for your tremendous forgiveness”. The omission of the verb thus shortening the sentence to use fewer words is employed in Arabic when something is desperate or urgent.
Derivative: تُحَمِّلْ means to pile burdens i.e., one on top of another.
Derivative: اغْفِرْ is the command form of غَفَرَ which means to forgive and implies the meaning of covering something i.e., to cover up sins.
Derivative: انصُرْ is the command form of نَصَرَ – نَصْر or نُصْرَة and means to provide aid and assistance for major matters like to overcome an enemy or oppressor.
Derivative: اعْفُ is the command form of عَفا – يَعْفُو – عَفْو which means to pardon and implies pardoning that is undertaken out of lovingness.

- It (i.e., the Nafs – Person) will have (لَهَا) what it earned (مَا كَسَبَتْ) and it will have against it (وَعَلَيْهَا) what it has earned (مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ)”. Here the verb كَسَبَ (Kasaba) is used for what one earns of good and is in his favour whilst the verb اِكْتَسَبَ (Iktasaba) is used for what the person earns which is bad and against him. The verb اِكْتَسَبَ (Iktasaba) is an earning that requires more effort than the verb كَسَبَ (Kasaba) and this implies that good deeds can be earned easier than earning bad deeds.
details.arabic_part: لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ

details.text: Derivative: جَمِيعًا means “all” or “everything”. It can refer to back to the لَكُمْ “For you” in the statement with the meaning “He it is who created for you “all” whatever is on the earth” or it can refer back to مَّا فِي الْأَرْضِ “Whatever is on the earth” with the overall meaning of “He it is who created for you everything of what is on the earth”.

- يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلَاةَ “They establish the Salah” – يُقِيمُونَ is from أّقَامَ which means to establish something. يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلَاةَ therefore doesn’t just mean to perform the Salah, but rather to establish it firmly.

Derivative: خَلِيفَةً has been said to refer to someone who will be succeeded and refers to the creation of humans who will succeed one another and come one after the after i.e., they will have children and generations will follow one after the other.
Derivative: وَإِذْ means “And when” however its understood meaning is: وَاذْكُرُوا إِذ “And remember when” implying: “Reflect upon the time when such and such an occurrence happened”.

- The phrase كُلَّهَا literally means “All of them” in reference to the names that Allah taught Adam (as), however كُلّ (Kull) in addition to “every” and “all” can also be used to imply “a lot of things” or “all kinds and variety of things” i.e., its usage does not necessarily imply absolutely everything without exception.

Derivative: سُبْحَانَ means “to declare the perfection”. It comes from the word سَبَحَ which means to swim on top of the water and it implies to maintain the level of perfection and perfect status without reducing it in the slightest.

- The pronoun هِم attached to أَسْمَاء (names) which is used to refer to the plural of humans or those possessing minds (عُقول) instead of هَا which is used for plural objects is because the أَسْمَاء (names) although predominantly referring to objects can also include the knowledge of names of people, nations etc. i.e. those who possess minds.
Derivative: أَبَىٰ means to refuse and is specifically used when you refuse to obey the authority that is over you.
Derivative: اسْتَكْبَرَ literally means to seek greatness and implies pride linked to arrogance.
Derivative: اسْكُنْ comes from the word سُكون (Sukoon) which means to become still and here it means to take as your home and to settle.

Derivative: مَتَاعٌ in Arabic refers to a utensil, tool or household item that you use in everyday life. In can also refer to your baggage and goods. It refers to that you make use of and which you gain utility and benefit from.
Derivative: مُسْتَقَرٌّ is a place that you find stability and peace of mind in.

- “Then Shaytan made them slip from it (عَنْهَا). Then he caused them both to exit or he expelled them (فَأَخْرَجَهُمَا) from what they were in (مِمَّا كَانَا فِيهِ)” – “From it” (عَنْهَا) can refer to Jannah or the tree. In case of the tree then it implies “because of it”. “He caused them to exit” (فَأَخْرَجَهُمَا) can be attributed to Shaytan in the case that he was the cause for them being expelled from Jannah or to Allah as He was the One who actually expelled them. “From what they were in” مِمَّا كَانَا فِيهِ can mean in terms of نَعِيم (Na’eem – Bliss).
details.arabic_part: فَأَزَلَّهُمَا الشَّيْطَانُ عَنْهَا فَأَخْرَجَهُمَا مِمَّا كَانَا فِيهِ

details.text: Derivative: التَّوَّابُ is from تَابَ which means to return or turn back to. It is used to refer to returning to Allah in repentance or for Allah turning to his slaves to accept their repentance. التَّوَّابُ (At-Tawwaab) means the one who turns to accept repentance. It is in the فَعَّال (Fa’’aal) form which indicates that Allah turns to his slaves to accept their repentance over and over again i.e., Allah continuously accepts Tawbah (تَوْبَة).
Derivative: الرَّحِيمُ (Ar-Raheem) is the فَعِيل (Fa’eel) form and means constantly merciful.

Derivative: كَلِمَاتٍ (Kalimaat) means words and refers to the words that Adam (as) received from his Lord to teach him how to repent. These words are mentioned in Surah Al-Aa’raaf Aayah 23: رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ “O our Lord, we have wronged ourselves and if you do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be from the losers”.

- “Then Adam received (فَتَلَقَّىٰ آدَمُ) from his Lord (مِن رَّبِّهِ) words (كَلِمَاتٍ)” – Here the normal Arabic sentence structure would be: “Then Adam received words from his Lord” however مِن رَّبِّهِ “From his Lord” has been placed earlier in the sentence structure and this serves to provide the implication that “Adam received words “especially” from his Lord” which implies Allah’s favour to him.

- لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ – “There will be no fear upon them”. The use of لَا خَوْفٌ (Laa Khawfun) “No fear” is not an absolute negation of fear like the statement لَا خَوْفَ (Laa Khawfa) would imply. Rather, it implies that most of the fear will be removed or that their fear will be a lot less than others. In addition, there is a difference between someone fearing and for there to be a fear upon or over someone. The fear of believers is mentioned multiple times in the Qur’an and commended. Its meaning is therefore that there is nothing for them to fear in terms of consequences or that there is no fear concerning them.

- It literally translates as “And they, them (هُمْ), will not grieve”- The هُمْ (them) placed here after the لا and the verb being negated يَحْزَنُونَ implies the meaning: “It is not them who will be grieving” i.e., it will be others who will be grieving. يَحْزَنُونَ (they grieve) is a verb which when used implies temporariness as opposed to a noun. This can be linked to the temporary nature of grief as opposed to the permanent nature of fear (خَوْف - Khawf) mentioned before it.

Derivative: يُوقِنُونَ comes from أَيْقَنَ – إِيقَان means to be convinced of something to such a level that it is almost as if you can see it i.e., to be thoroughly convinced. Others have provided it with a stronger meaning than the above and stated that it refers to the decisive belief that conforms to reality in the case where no doubt at all can enter it and no questions of doubt can be raised concerning it. It therefore refers to an absolute conviction in which there is no doubt and something that is known as a fact.

- “And in the hereafter (وَبِالْآخِرَةِ) they are thoroughly convinced (هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ)” – The fact that بِالْآخِرَةِ “In the hereafter” has been advance in the sentence structure and placed before the verb it implies an extra meaning as follows: “And especially in the hereafter, they are thoroughly convinced”.

Derivative: أَوَّلَ (Awwal) means “first” but does not necessitate the meaning of being first in order but can also imply being the foremost in something i.e., don’t be in the front of the line or take pole position in being disbelievers.

Derivative: تَعْقِلُونَ comes from the verb عَقَلَ (‘Aqala) which in origin means “to tie” and was used to describe a woman tying up her hair. عِقال (‘Iqaal) is a rope or cord used to tie a camel up or to hold up the Arab headdress or turban. The same word of عَقْل (‘Aql) is used for intellect because for the Arabs the word ‘Aql showed that you had control and restraint. Having control over emotions means that you have ‘Aql (intellect). تَعْقِلُونَ implies the meaning is using your minds correctly.

Derivative: اسْتَعِينُوا is the command form from the word اِسْتَعَانَ – يَسْتَعِينُ – اِسْتِعَانَة. اِسْتِعَانَة (Isti’aanah) in turn comes from the word عَوْن (‘Awn) which means assistance and help. اِسْتِعَانَة (Isti’aanah) means to seek assistance and to ask for help in the situation when someone is already engaged in an act and in the effort to finish a task but is unable to complete it by himself.
Derivative: الصَّبْر – Sabr includes the meanings of patience, perseverance, constancy and steadfastness.

Derivative: الْخَاشِعِينَ (Al-Khaashi’een) means those who have awe of Allah and humble themselves to Him. The meaning of the description of الْخَاشِعِينَ (Al-Khaashi’een) mentioned here is elaborated upon in the Aayah that follows.
Derivative: لَكَبِيرَةٌ refers to something that is big or great in magnitude and an enormity i.e., a difficult and heavy matter or task.

- The إِنَّهَا (No doubt, it) here in the statement وَإِنَّهَا لَكَبِيرَةٌ إِلَّا عَلَى الْخَاشِعِينَ “And no doubt, it (إِنَّهَا) is an enormity except for the Khaashi’een” refers either to the Salah which is mentioned before it or it refers to the verb at the beginning اسْتَعِينُوا “Seek help”.

Derivative: يَظُنُّونَ is from ظَنَّ – ظَنٌّ and is a word in Arabic that carries opposite meanings. ظَنٌّ (Zhann) can refer to an assumption that is not based on any conviction and at the same time it can mean to have the strongest possible conviction. Here it is the latter meaning in the case where يَظُنُّونَ means “to be thoroughly and absolutely convinced”.

Derivative: مُّلَاقُو “They will meet” – Instead of the verb to meet, a noun is used which implies that their belief that they will meet their Lord is one that is certain and a permanently fixed belief in their minds.

Derivative: الْعَالَمِينَ “Al-‘Aalameen” is in the sound masculine plural form which has been given to عَالَم (world). The normal plural for عَالَم (‘Aalam) is عَوَالِم (‘Awaalim). The sound masculine plural is used when those possessing an intellect (‘Aql) are referred to and as such the meaning of الْعَالَمِينَ “Al-‘Aalameen” here can refer to the peoples and nations of the world.

- فَضَّلْتُكُمْ means “I gave you preference” or “I favoured you”. It includes “I” in its meaning and the repetition of the pronoun of “I” in أَنِّي “Indeed, I” is used for more than the purpose of emphasis. It is called الإِثْبَات للْفَاعِل (Al-Ithbaat Lil-Faa’il – The reinforcement of the doer of the action). The implied meaning here would be: “It is in fact I who gave you preference or who favoured you” which negates that anyone else did it.
Derivative: عَدْلٌ refers to a ransom or a compensation that you offer to ransom oneself with.

- “And intercession will not be accepted from it (i.e., the Nafs – Person)” - The fact that يُقْبَلُ which is the masculine verb form is used instead of the feminine form تُقْبَلُ implies that it won’t just be the شَفَاعَةٌ (intercession) which won’t be accepted but is referring to a more encompassing meaning in terms of also including the meaning that the شَفَاعَةٌ (intercession) of others on your behalf will also not be accepted.
Derivative: نَجَّيْنَا is from نَجَّى which means to save or rescue. Its verb form indicates to the repetition of the action i.e., We saved or rescued you on multiple occasions.
Derivative: يَسُومُونَ is from سَامَ which means to make someone taste a punishment, to impose it upon them and implies the meaning of humiliating that person.

Derivative: الْمُفْلِحُونَ is the plural of مُفْلِح (Muflih). It comes from the verb أَفْلَحَ and means the one who is successful. The word in origin relates to the farmer who after undertaking many actions over a lengthy period of time with his crops reaps the fruit of his harvest i.e., he succeeds. The word therefore implies the meaning of attaining success after a long time of labour and making efforts to achieve that success.

Derivative: أَنجَيْنَا like نَجَّيْنَا means to save and rescue however its form from أَنْجَى implies that the saving was complete with there being no more need for being rescued after that from Fir’awn.
Derivative: تَنظُرُونَ is from نَظَرَ and means to look. It is used for looking at a thing that has drawn attention and implies staring.

- “From (مِن) after him (بَعْدِهِ)” – The مِن “from” preceding بَعْدِهِ “after him” adds the element of astonishment (At-Ta’ajjub) and implies disappointment. The meaning therefore resembles “Even after him” i.e., even after Musa (as) came to you, you still did this act.
Derivative: عَفَوْنَا “We pardoned” from عًفْو which means to pardon and forgive. It refers to a genuine and wholesome pardoning and not one that is done begrudgingly.

Derivative: الْفُرْقَانَ (Al-Furqaan) comes from فَرَقَ which means to separate and distinguish between two things in the case where there is a large and clear gap separating them. الْفُرْقَانَ (Al-Furqaan) implies a Book that has come to make sure that you understand the right from the wrong, where there is a clear gap between the two.
Derivative: الْكِتَابَ means “The Book” which can refer to the Tawrah and it can also be a reference to “The law”.

Derivative: تَهْتَدُونَ is from اِهْتَدَى which is from the اِفْتَعَلَ (Ifta’ala) verb form. It means to be guided and its form implies that some effort be put into the act i.e., to make an effort towards guidance and to be guided.

- “We will never نُّؤْمِنَ لَكَ” – نُّؤْمِنَ comes from the verb آمَنَ. آمَنَ بِ means “to believe in something” however, آمَنَ لِ means to follow, comply and obey. The meaning of لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكَ is therefore: “We will never follow and obey you” i.e., we will never submit to your commands.
Derivative: السَّلْوَىٰ (As-Salwaa) refers to a small bird that is easy to hunt and has good and nutritious meat to consume.
Derivative: الْغَمَامَ refers to the clouds that are white, not rain clouds and provide covering from the sun.
Derivative: الْمَنَّ (Al-Manna) is a special source of food (given to Bani Isra’eel) referring to a substance or grain that could be used to make food which provides nourishment.
Derivative: طَيِّبَاتِ (Tayyibaat) – The plural of طَيِّب (Tayyib) which refers to something good, pure, wholesome and lawful.

Derivative: حِطَّةٌ (Hittah) comes from حَطَّ which means to put down or bring down like when a load is taken down from a donkey. حِطَّةٌ (Hittah) implies “We are burdened with a load of sins and so take that load off of us”. It implies dropping a load or relieving a burden in the case where the load and burden was their sins.
Derivative: كَفَرُوا – “They have disbelieved” is used in the past tense and implies those who have cemented their disbelief and are closed to receiving guidance or accepting the message.
Derivative: انفَجَرَتْ is from اِنْفِجَار which means to explode and burst open in reference to the water gushing forth.
Derivative: تَعْثَوْ from عَثَا or عَثِيَ which means to act very corruptly and to cause great corruption.
Derivative: مُفْسِدِينَ (Mufsideen) refers to those who spread corruption and are a cause for corruption.

Derivative: مِصْرًا has been interpreted with two meanings. One is that it is the meaning of مِصْر (Misr) that means a region in which case it would be referring to any area where people are settled. The use of the Tanween ً indicates to this meaning in the case where مِصْرُ (Misr) referring to the place of Egypt does not have a Tanween. It has also been stated that it refers to Egypt in which case the context of the statement would be: “If you are not happy then return to where you came from (i.e., Egypt) where you had the things that you are asking for”. Even in the case of the first meaning of it referring to any unspecified region or area where people are settled, the specific choice of using the word مِصْر (Misr) here could imply that it was used specifically as a reminder for where they (i.e., Bani Isra’eel) came from and what Allah saved them from.
Derivative: عَصَوا is from عَصَى – يَعْصِي - عِصْيَان which means to disobey. It is disobedience associated with arrogance and defiance.
Derivative: آيَاتِ (signs) refers to two things here: The first is the revelations and the second is the miracles.

Derivative: تَتَّقُونَ means “You (plural) attain تَقْوَى (Taqwa)”. تَقْوَى (Taqwa) includes is understood in two ways. One is that it refers to protecting yourself from destruction and the other is that you attain Taqwaa according to its general meaning and become from the those are Al-Muttaqoon (المُتَّقُون).

Derivative: مِيثَاقَ refers to an agreement or covenant. It implies an agreement that is laid out clearly and carries with it the undertaking of certain responsibilities. It also refers to something the seriousness and gravity of which is required to be taken into account.
Derivative: اذْكُرُوا is from ذَكَرَ which implies two meanings: One is to remember and the other is to mention.
Derivative: إِنَّ carries the meaning of “certainly” or “For sure” and is used for specific rhetorical purposes in the Arabic language and its function is to remove doubt.

Derivative: الْجَاهِلِينَ comes from جَهَالَة (Jahaalah) which is ignorance. جَاهِل (Jaahil) is the opposite of عَاقِل (‘Aaqil) which refers to the one who is capable of controlling his emotions and maintains reason and mind. The جَاهِل (Jaahil) is the one who does not have or employ restraint in his actions or statements.

Derivative: افْعَلُوا “Do”. A فِعْل (act) in contrast to عَمَل (an act) does not require thought and it implies here that what they have been commanded with does not require any thinking about it, rather افْعَلُوا “Just do it”.
Derivative: فَاقِعٌ in refers to a texture of colour that is deep, intense and brilliant i.e., vivid and eye-catching.

- “And upon their vision (or eyes) (وَعَلَىٰ أَبْصَارِهِمْ) is a covering (غِشَاوَةٌ)” – This covering over their eyes means that although they can see, they still can’t see i.e., they don’t see the truth despite being so clear.

Derivative: ادَّارَأْتُمْ from اِدَّارَأَ which is in origin from the تَفاعَلَ verb form and literally means to throw and cast something at one another. Here it refers to casting accusations at one another. ادَّارَأْتُمْ means “You (plural) cast or hurl accusations at each other”.
Derivative: غَافِلٍ refers to the one who acts in an unaware, oblivious or heedless manner.

Derivative: أَمَانِيَّ (Amaaniy) is the plural of أُمْنِيَة (Umniyah) and it refers to wishful thoughts. The wishful thoughts here can apply to the lies that they are fed by their leaders and religious figures.
Derivative: يَظُنُّونَ from ظَنَّ means that they make assumptions or suppositions i.e., reach thoughts and conclusions based on other than evidence and true knowledge.

Derivative: أُمِّيُّونَ is the plural of أُمِّيّ (Umiyy) which means two things: The inability to read and the inability to write. It comes from theWord: أُمٌّ (Umm) which means mother. It means that they are as illiterate as they were when they first came out of their mother. It has also been interpreted to refer here to the “masses” or “general and common people” (العَوامّAl-‘Awaamm) who follow and imitate their leaders and not people who don’t have the actual ability to read and write.
Derivative: الْكِتَابَ refers to their scripture and can also refer to “law” in the language.
Derivative: وَيْلٌ Refers to the most terrible destruction and punishment.

- When مَا is combined with a بِ attached to the noun مَا is negating it represents one of the strongest ways in Arabic for emphasising the negation to imply the meaning “And they are not believers at all”.

وَقَالُوا لَن تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَةً

- “And they said: Never will the fire (of hell) touch us except for a few days”. This is an example of the wishful thoughts (أَمَانِيَّ (Amaaniy)) that they had built for themselves mentioned two verses previously in Al-Baqarah: 78.

Derivative: أَحَاطَتْ from أَحَاطَ means to be encircled and here in respect to a sin, it implies being consumed and overtaken by it where it has taken control over the person, like an addiction. Being engulfed (أَحَاطَتْ) in that sin can imply its spiralling effect in terms of propelling the person into greater and mor widespread sinful behaviour.
Derivative: سَيِّئَةً refers to a sinful act and only refers to those which are serious in contrast to the word ذَنْب (Dhanb) which refers to that which could be large or small.

وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ

- “And those who (وَالَّذِينَ) have believed (آمَنُوا) and done (وَعَمِلُوا) righteous acts (الصَّالِحَاتِ), those are (أُولَٰئِكَ) the companions (أَصْحَابُ) of Paradise (الْجَنَّةِ)” – This statement lays out the formula for success: Belief and righteous deeds are the two steps or conditions that lead to Paradise. Belief without righteous deeds does not lead to Paradise just as good deeds without belief does not lead to the attainment of Paradise.

- Literally this means: “You will not spill or shed your blood (دِمَاءَكُمْ) and you will not expel yourselves (أَنفُسَكُم) from your homes (دِيَارِكُمْ)” – Here the blood is being described as your own, those being expelled as your own selves and the homes as your own homes. The meaning however is to not shed the blood of each other and do not expel each other from their homes. The use of “your” blood, “your” selves and “your” homes implies that when you do it to others from your community or Ummah it is like you are doing it to your own selves.
details.arabic_part: لَا تَسْفِكُونَ دِمَاءَكُمْ وَلَا تُخْرِجُونَ أَنفُسَكُم مِّن دِيَارِكُمْ

details.text: Derivative: كَذَّبْتُمْ from كَذَّبَ – تَكْذِيب which means to declare something to be a lie or false. In respect to the Prophets, it means denying them, their message and commands by declaring them to be false or imposters.

Derivative: اسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ from اِسْتَكْبَرَ which literally means to seek greatness and it implies that you will not let anything get in the way of you achieving your desire for greatness and the fulfilment of your desires. It implies the arrogance and pride that prevents someone from following the truth or the correct path.
Derivative: قَفَّيْنَا – قَفَا (Qafaa) means to follow and قَفَّا (Qaffaa) means to make follow or to send someone after someone else.

Derivative: The لَام (Laam - لَ) in لَقَدْ is اللَّامُ جَوابُ القَسْم (Al-Laam Jawaab ul-Qasm) and it is used for the purposes of emphasis, affirmation and confirmation. It is used to give greater emphasis to the meaning of the verb and is used particularly with the past tense.
- رُوحِ الْقُدُسِ (Roohi l-Qudus) refers to Jibreel (as) in the case where الْقُدُسِ refers to being purified and sanctified.

Derivative: غُلْفٌ means to be wrapped up and covered by a covering. In relation to their hearts its implies that they are closed to hearing and understanding what is said to them as they arrogantly believe themselves not in need of it and self-sufficient.
Derivative: يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ from اِسْتَفْتَحَ which is used with عَلَى (‘Alaa) to mean to “supplicate to Allah for victory over (someone)”.
Derivative: بَغْيًا means “out of rebellion” i.e., they do such and such بَغْيًا “Out of or due to rebellion”. بَغَي refers to the desire to dominate and not be subservient to anyone else.

وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَاقَكُمْ وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَكُمُ الطُّورَ خُذُوا مَا آتَيْنَاكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَاسْمَعُوا ۖ قَالُوا سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا وَأُشْرِبُوا فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْعِجْلَ بِكُفْرِهِمْ ۚ قُلْ بِئْسَمَا يَأْمُرُكُم بِهِ إِيمَانُكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

- The transition from using the second person form in مِيثَاقَكُمْ (Your covenant), فَوْقَكُمُ (Above you) and آتَيْنَاكُم “We gave you”, to the third person قَالُوا (They said) represents تَبْعِيد (distancing) which is used when you are disgusted by someone’s behaviour and choose to address them indirectly. Then when it returns to the second person it is not directly from Allah but rather Allah instructs the Messenger ﷺ to say to them, when He said: قُلْ (Qul – Say).

- أُشْرِبُوا means to be made to be saturated and filled. The statement states: That something related to the الْعِجْلَ (Al-‘Ijl – Calf) was saturated in their hearts due to their disbelief”. The implied something here which is not explicitly mentioned is the meaning of “love” i.e., the “love” of the calf was saturated within their hearts.

- Because of what (بِمَا) their hands (أَيْدِيهِمْ) have sent forward (قَدَّمَتْ) – This is a Quranic expression in reference to what someone has invested in terms of the actions he has undertaken, the results and consequences of which he will see later.
Derivative: بَيِّنَاتٍ is the plural of بَيِّنَة (Bayyinah) which refers to a clear undeniable proof.
